- Raises GH / IGF-1 in humans: grade B. Evidence base: Phase 1 (Teichman 2006) + animal studies. Reported effect: Sustained elevation. Caveat: Pharmacology is real; but raising GH/IGF-1 ≠ a proven clinical benefit.
- Clinical efficacy (lipodystrophy): grade D. Evidence base: Phase 2 (NCT00267527) — halted. Reported effect: Inconclusive. Caveat: Trial terminated after a participant death; no efficacy conclusion reached.
- Anti-aging / body composition / muscle: grade F. Evidence base: None in humans. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Extrapolated from GH/IGF-1 changes; never tested for these goals.
- Safety & long-term: grade not graded. Evidence base: Limited; a trial-death shadow. Reported effect: Unknown. Caveat: Causation disputed; GH/IGF-1 elevation carries theoretical risks (IGF-1/oncologic, insulin resistance, edema); WADA-banned.
Legal status (EU): Not approved. Not approved; not eligible for magistral/officinal compounding (no Ph. Eur. monograph).
Legal status (US): Not FDA-approved (503A Cat. 2). Placed in 503A Category 2 (2023); its status is in flux amid the 2026 HHS/FDA peptide reclassification, but it is not among the seven peptides in the specific July 23–24 2026 PCAC review; not FDA-approved (IND).
Legal status (INT): Not approved. Not approved.
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — GHRH analogs / GH secretagogues fall under Section S2.2.
Research / reference — not for sale. No dosing, reconstitution, or administration is published (intentional). Neutral scientific reference only. A GH secretagogue with a real (and cautionary) clinical-development history.
Evidence provenance: 3 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/cjc-1295 (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: CJC-1295 + Ipamorelin ("CJC/Ipa")
title: CJC-1295 + Ipamorelin — the "CJC/Ipa" stack, evidence & status
text:
CJC-1295 + Ipamorelin ("CJC/Ipa") — blend.
Also known as: "CJC/Ipa", "GHRH + GHRP stack", GH-secretagogue combination. INCI: none.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Synergistic GH release (combination): grade C. Evidence base: Mechanistic (GHRH + GHRP synergy is established); each component raises GH in humans. Reported effect: Larger GH pulse than either alone (plausible). Caveat: Mechanistically sound, but see below — the specific combo is essentially untested in controlled trials.
- The specific CJC-1295 + Ipamorelin combination for any clinical endpoint: grade F. Evidence base: No controlled clinical trial has evaluated it. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Zero combination RCT; anti-aging/body-composition claims are extrapolation.
- Safety (combination): grade not graded. Evidence base: No dedicated human safety data. Reported effect: Unknown. Caveat: Inherits both components' unknowns; GH/IGF-1 elevation theoretical risks; WADA-banned.
Legal status (INT): See note. the status of both components applies — placed in 503A Category 2 (2023), status in flux in 2026, not among the July 23–24 2026 PCAC seven, not FDA-approved; not approved in the EU; WADA-prohibited (S2.2).
WADA: prohibited in sport. WADA-prohibited (S2
Research / reference — not for sale. No dosing, reconstitution, or administration is published (intentional). A combination, not a single molecule — and the most-marketed GH-peptide stack with the least combination-specific evidence.
Honest note: this is the clearest "popularity ≠ evidence" case in the register — the most-marketed GH stack, yet no controlled trial has ever tested the combination. What is known is the pharmacology of each part (they raise GH), not that the stack delivers the outcomes it is sold for.
Evidence provenance: 0 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.

name: Dasatinib + Quercetin / Fisetin (Senolytics)
title: Dasatinib + Quercetin & Fisetin — the first senolytics in human trials: how "zombie cell" clearance really works, and what the data show
text:
Dasatinib + Quercetin / Fisetin (Senolytics) — blend.
Also known as: D+Q (Dasatinib + Quercetin), Fisetin (a separate flavonoid senolytic), "senolytics" (from senescence + lytic).
Dasatinib+Quercetin (D+Q) and Fisetin are the first and best-known senolytics — drugs that selectively kill senescent "zombie" cells, which accumulate with age and pump out an inflammatory secretome (SASP) implicated in 40+ age-related conditions. They were discovered by a clever hypothesis (Kirkland/Mayo, 2015): senescent cells cling to life via anti-apoptotic survival pathways, so blocking those makes them self-destruct while normal cells are spared. Dasatinib is an FDA-approved leukemia drug; quercetin (apple-peel bitterness) and fisetin (most concentrated in strawberries) are natural flavonoids — fisetin being the most potent and safest-profiled. A key trick is "hit-and-run" dosing — brief pulses (about two days every couple of weeks) that clear senescent cells while dodging a chemo drug's sustained toxicity. Their decisive distinction in this register: they actually reached human trials — the first to demonstrably reduce senescent cells in people (2019, diabetic kidney disease). But across Alzheimer's, osteoporosis, and frailty studies, the clinical outcomes have been mixed and modest — real cell-clearing, few clear wins, small underfunded trials, and missing biomarkers. The honest read: the most clinically-advanced senolytics — genuinely in humans, genuinely clearing zombie cells — but not yet proven to deliver the healthspan benefits the mouse data promise. Powerful proof-of-concept; unfinished clinical story.
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Senescent-cell clearance in humans (the proof-of-concept): grade B. Evidence base: Hickson 2019 (diabetic kidney disease): D+Q measurably reduced senescent cells in people. Caveat: The genuine landmark — first human senolytic clearance; but clearance ≠ clinical benefit.
- Lifespan/healthspan (animal): grade A. Evidence base: D+Q and fisetin extend healthspan/lifespan across mouse models; fisetin top of 10 flavonoids. Caveat: Strong mouse data — the usual "mouse ≠ human" gap applies.
- Alzheimer's / cognition (human): grade F. Evidence base: SToMP-AD Phase 1: "very few significant results, no suggestion of effectiveness"; STAMINA pilot (n=12) feasibility only. Caveat: Tested — no cognitive benefit shown; safety/feasibility only.
- Osteoporosis / bone (human): grade D. Evidence base: Mayo Phase 2: missed on whole group; burden-dependent subgroup signal. Caveat: Negative primary; hypothesis that baseline senescent burden dictates response.
- Frailty / physical function (human): grade C. Evidence base: Fisetin AFFIRM-LITE: mixed, some positive physical-function trends. Caveat: Preliminary/mixed; ongoing.
- Safety (intermittent dosing): grade B. Evidence base: Short "hit-and-run" pulses safe/well-tolerated in trials to date. Caveat: Flavonoids benign; dasatinib is a real chemo drug — off-label self-use is hazardous.
Legal status (INT): See note. - Dasatinib — an approved prescription cancer drug (leukemia). Senolytic/longevity use is off-label and requires medical oversight (it's a real chemotherapeutic).
- Quercetin / Fisetin — natural flavonoids sold as dietary supplements (widely legal); found in foods. No approved senolytic/anti-aging indication.
- Your region · EU · US — no senolytic use is approved; all longevity use is investigational/off-label.
- Gray-market note: quercetin and fisetin are freely sold supplements (and cosmetic ingredients); dasatinib is a potent prescription chemo drug that should never be self-administered for longevity.
The "zombie cell" killers that actually reached the clinic — the honest counterpoint to FOXO4-DRI. This closing entry is the register's real-world senolytic case, and its whole value is the contrast with FOXO4-DRI (#67): where that peptide has zero human data, Dasatinib+Quercetin (D+Q) and Fisetin have been through dozens of human trials. Three threads: (1) they were the first senolytics ever discovered — via a clever hypothesis, not random screening; (2) they clear senescent "zombie" cells using a short "hit-and-run" dosing that sidesteps a cancer drug's toxicity; (3) crucially, the human results so far are mixed and modest — real senescent-cell clearance, but few clear clinical wins. Dasatinib is a prescription cancer drug; quercetin and fisetin are natural flavonoids.
Status note: the field's need: adequately-powered Phase 2/3 trials with validated senescence biomarkers — many ongoing (sepsis, PAD, osteoarthritis); the open question is whether human clearance finally translates to clinical benefit
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 3 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/dasatinib-quercetin (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Dihexa (PNB-0408)
title: Dihexa — the "10-million-times-BDNF" nootropic peptide: retractions, zero human data, cancer-pathway risk
text:
Dihexa (PNB-0408) — peptide.
Also known as: Dihexa, PNB-0408, N-hexanoic-Tyr-Ile-(6)-aminohexanoic amide, derived from Nle¹-angiotensin IV. MW ≈ 504.67. INCI: none.
Dihexa is a synthetic angiotensin-IV-derived peptide engineered to be orally active and cross into the brain, studied as a synapse-building (synaptogenic) cognitive enhancer via the HGF/c-Met growth-factor pathway. It became famous on a spectacular claim — ~10 million times more potent than BDNF at driving synaptogenesis in slice assays — which made it a nootropic-underground legend. The honest picture is sobering: all evidence is preclinical (cells and rodents), there are zero human trials, and the foundational mechanism papers were retracted in 2025 for fabricated data, with the responsible scientist (Leen Kawas) found "solely responsible" alongside the lab head. That same scientist ran Athira Pharma, whose related HGF/c-Met drug fosgonimeton failed its Alzheimer's trials — the only clinical test of this lineage, and it flopped. Add no safety data, a very long half-life, and the fact that HGF/c-Met is a validated cancer driver, and Dihexa becomes the register's clearest cautionary tale: an extraordinary claim resting on a collapsed foundation. It closes the cognitive cluster as the opposite of Cerebrolysin (#43) — not "lots of contested data," but "almost no legitimate data at all."
Overall evidence grade: F (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Synaptogenesis / cognition (the marketed use): grade F. Evidence base: Preclinical only: rodent cognitive-restoration (scopolamine/aged) + slice synaptogenesis; "10M× BDNF" claim. Caveat: Zero human data; the headline potency claim traces to retracted, fabricated work.
- HGF/c-Met mechanism: grade F. Evidence base: Original binding paper (Kd 65 pM) retracted 2025; some independent HGF/c-Met corroboration (Sun 2021). Caveat: Best treated as an unproven working hypothesis, not established fact.
- Clinical translation (via the lineage): grade F. Evidence base: The related drug fosgonimeton failed ACT-AD + LIFT-AD (no better than placebo). Caveat: The one clinical program on this pathway/lineage failed — a strong negative signal.
- Human efficacy or safety (Dihexa itself): grade F. Evidence base: None. Caveat: No trials, no safety profile, very long duration → adverse effects not rapidly reversible.
- Safety / oncologic risk: grade not graded. Evidence base: Pathway-level concern. Caveat: HGF/c-Met is a validated cancer-driver pathway; chronically potentiating it for enhancement is a real theoretical risk, unquantified in humans.
Legal status (EU): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). not approved anywhere; investigational research chemical. Not a cosmetic. No compounding pathway (reports indicate FDA removed it from interim Category-2 bulk-substance consideration).
The most hyped nootropic peptide — and the register's starkest "spectacular claims, collapsed foundation" case. Three points define this entry, and they close the cognitive cluster opposite Cerebrolysin (#43): (1) it rode a jaw-dropping claim — "10 million times more potent than BDNF" at building synapses; (2) its foundational mechanism papers were retracted in 2025 for fabricated data, and the scientist responsible went on to run a biotech whose related drug failed its Alzheimer's trials; (3) it has zero human data and targets a pathway (HGF/c-Met) that is a validated cancer driver. Where Cerebrolysin is "most-studied but contested," Dihexa is "most-hyped but hollow."
Status note: status: preclinical + retracted foundation + failed related clinical program; unlikely to improve without new, independent human data
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/dihexa (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: DSIP (Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide / Emideltide)
title: DSIP (Emideltide) — evidence, the discovery story & status
text:
DSIP (Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide / Emideltide) — peptide.
Also known as: DSIP, Delta Sleep-Inducing Peptide, Emideltide (the name the FDA uses in the Federal Register), nonapeptide, sequence Trp-Ala-Gly-Gly-Asp-Ala-Ser-Gly-Glu (WAGGDASGE), CAS 62568-57-4. INCI: none.
DSIP (regulatory name Emideltide) is a naturally occurring nonapeptide named for its ability to promote delta (deep-sleep) EEG in rabbits. But despite roughly 50 years of study, the human evidence that it reliably induces sleep is limited, mixed and contradictory — the best-designed trial found only weak effects — its mechanism and even its receptor are unknown, and it is not approved anywhere. The name is more definitive than the data.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Delta / slow-wave EEG (animal): grade C. Evidence base: Monnier & Schoenenberger 1977 (rabbit). Reported effect: Increased delta activity. Caveat: The original finding; animal, and even here later work was mixed.
- Chronic insomnia (human): grade C. Evidence base: Small 1980s studies (Schneider-Helmert) + Bes et al. 1992. Reported effect: Mixed; some positive, but the strongest trial showed only weak effects. Caveat: Human evidence is limited and contradictory — the "sleep-inducing" name overpromises.
- Opioid / alcohol withdrawal, stress, pain: grade D. Evidence base: Older, small studies. Reported effect: Suggestive. Caveat: Thin; not replicated to modern standards.
- Mechanism / receptor: grade not graded. Evidence base: —. Reported effect: Unknown. Caveat: No confirmed receptor; gene not identified; possible bacterial origin.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: No modern human safety RCT. Reported effect: Unknown. Caveat: Long-term safety not established.
Legal status (EU): Not approved. Not approved; not eligible for magistral/officinal compounding.
Legal status (US): Not FDA-approved (503A Cat. 2). Not FDA-approved. Placed in 503A Category 2 (Sept 2023); scheduled for PCAC review in July 2026 under the name Emideltide, covering proposed uses in opioid withdrawal, chronic insomnia and narcolepsy (docket FDA-2025-N-6895). A change in review status — not an approval.
Legal status (INT): Approved nowhere. Approved nowhere.
Research / reference — not for sale, not approved. The honest crux here: the name is a claim the human evidence doesn't robustly support. ~50 years of study, mixed and contradictory results, and — remarkably — no known receptor or gene. No dosing published here.
Status note: US review status evolving — re-check the July PCAC/Emideltide outcome
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
