name: Ectoin
title: Ectoin — evidence, uses & status
text:
Ectoin — small_molecule.
Also known as: (S)-2-methyl-1,4,5,6-tetrahydropyrimidine-4-carboxylic acid, THP(B) — tetrahydropyrimidine, extremolyte, INCI: Ectoin.
Ectoin is a small (142 Da) amino-acid-derived "extremolyte" — a molecule that desert bacteria in salt lakes manufacture to survive extreme heat, UV, and osmotic stress. On skin it works as an osmolyte, forming a protective water shell around cells that stabilises the barrier, reduces transepidermal water loss, calms the appearance of redness, and defends against environmental stressors. It's exceptionally gentle (hypoallergenic, pregnancy-friendly) and — unlike many trend molecules — it's small enough to genuinely penetrate skin. Honest limit: ectoin protects and hydrates, but does not build collagen or replace retinol. It's a partner, not a hero.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Topical: barrier function / TEWL reduction: grade B. Evidence base: Multiple placebo-controlled clinical trials over 20+ years; consistent TEWL reductions of 15–72% depending on skin state and formulation. Reported effect: Measurable reduction in transepidermal water loss; barrier stabilisation. Caveat: Effect size varies with vehicle and baseline barrier state; well-established but not head-to-head vs ceramide-based formulas in most trials.
- Skin hydration: grade B. Evidence base: Controlled trials with 0.5–7% ectoin creams and lotions. Reported effect: Measurable increase in stratum corneum hydration; sustained over weeks of use. Caveat: Effect works via osmolyte mechanism (water-shell around cells) rather than humectant (like HA).
- Redness and appearance of sensitive/reactive skin: grade B. Evidence base: Controlled trials showing measurable reduction in visible erythema and subjective irritation scores; use in sensitive-skin adjacent categories. Reported effect: Reduction in appearance of redness (~38% in some trials). Caveat: Adjunct effect; not a treatment for underlying skin conditions.
- Atopic dermatitis (medical use, not cosmetic): grade B. Evidence base: Multiple clinical trials with 5.5–7% ectoin cream (branded medical device products); comparable to low-potency corticosteroid in some studies. Reported effect: Improvement in SCORAD and symptoms. Caveat: Medical device territory in EU, not cosmetic; do not conflate medical claims with topical cosmetic serum framing.
- Protection against UV and pollution-induced skin stress: grade B. Evidence base: In-vitro on skin cells and controlled human studies with UV/pollution exposure models. Reported effect: Measurable protection of cell viability and reduction of oxidative markers. Caveat: Not a sunscreen; adjunct to UV protection, not replacement.
- Anti-wrinkle / collagen-building claims: grade D. Evidence base: Limited direct evidence; effect largely inferred from barrier/hydration benefits. Reported effect: Modest at best, indirect. Caveat: Ectoin protects and hydrates; it does not build collagen or replace retinol. Framing as an anti-aging hero is a marketing overstatement..
- Safety (topical): grade not graded. Evidence base: Decades of cosmetic use; extensive tolerability data. Reported effect: Exceptionally well tolerated. Caveat: Hypoallergenic, pregnancy-friendly, no known conflicts with other cosmetic actives; suitable for infants and reactive skin.
Legal status (INT): Lawful cosmetic ingredient. Topical Ectoin is a lawful cosmetic ingredient globally, including EU (Regulation (EC) 1223/2009), UK, US, and Asia. CosIng-listed. Also registered as an active in EU medical-device products (e.g. atopic dermatitis creams) — these are regulated separately under MDR 2017/745, not as cosmetics. Consumer serum formulations typically use 0.5–2%.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): supports the appearance of a healthy skin barrier; for the appearance of hydrated, comfortable-feeling skin; helps reduce the look of redness; for the appearance of skin that feels calm and soothed; antioxidant.
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: treats atopic dermatitis; treats eczema; repairs the skin barrier; anti-inflammatory; reduces inflammation; reverses aging; builds collagen; replaces retinol.
Ectoin is a lawful cosmetic ingredient worldwide. Vallydia uses it in cosmetic serums at concentrations supported by clinical trials (0.5–2%) with appearance-only claims. Reference science below includes both topical cosmetic and medical-device uses (atopic dermatitis, rhinitis); only the cosmetic barrier/hydration/soothing-appearance framing is relevant to the sellable product.
Identity: a small (142 Da) cyclic amino-acid derivative — specifically (S)-2-methyl-1,4,5,6-tetrahydropyrimidine-4-carboxylic acid. Classified as an extremolyte: a natural molecule that certain microorganisms produce to survive extreme environmental stress (heat, cold, salinity, UV, desiccation). Extracted originally from Halomonas elongata, a bacterium isolated from the salt lakes of Wadi El Natrun, Egypt. Now produced industrially via biotechnology (bacterial fermentation) at high purity.
Mechanism (as proposed): a small, highly water-soluble molecule that binds a shell of ordered water molecules around itself and, when applied to skin, around cellular membranes and proteins. This water shell stabilises membranes and proteins under stress (heat, UV, osmotic shift, oxidative attack) without directly interacting with them — the mechanism is described as preferential exclusion. In the stratum corneum this translates to: reduced transepidermal water loss, stabilised barrier lipids, protection of keratinocytes from UV and pollution-induced damage, and reduced release of inflammatory mediators. Ectoin does not enzymatically modify collagen, elastin, or pigmentation pathways — its benefit is defensive and hydrating, not regenerative.
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 4 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/ectoin (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Eloralintide
title: Eloralintide — the selective amylin agonist with ~20% weight loss: the amylin story and what it means for the class
text:
Eloralintide — peptide.
Also known as: Eloralintide, LY3841136 (Lilly development code).
Eloralintide (Lilly's LY3841136) is a once-weekly, selective amylin-receptor agonist — and it closes the register's amylin story with the class's strongest monotherapy data yet. The amylin lineage has a lovely origin: the hormone was discovered as the mysterious amyloid "junk" clogging diabetics' pancreases for a century, which turned out to be a third islet hormone that signals fullness (co-secreted with insulin). Eloralintide's Phase 2 (Lancet, 2025) delivered up to ~20% mean weight loss over 48 weeks (range 9.5–20.1% vs 0.4% placebo) — incretin-class numbers from a non-incretin mechanism — with generally good tolerability (GI/fatigue AEs dose-dependent; placebo-like at lower doses). That result matters beyond the drug: arriving just after Petrelintide (#69) — whose ~10.7% disappointed the market — eloralintide shows the amylin class can reach the top tier, so efficacy magnitude, not just tolerability, is the real battleground. Lilly went straight to Phase 3 and is testing an eloralintide + tirzepatide combination. The honest read: a standout Phase 2 for a selective amylin agonist — high efficacy, good tolerability — but still investigational, Phase 3 pending, and (as Petrelintide showed) mid-stage promise isn't approval.
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Weight loss / obesity: grade B. Evidence base: Phase 2, Lancet 2025 (263 pts, 48 wk): up to 20.1% (−21.3 kg) vs 0.4% placebo; dose-dependent; Phase 1 ~11%/12 wk. Caveat: Standout Phase 2 — highest amylin-monotherapy weight loss reported — but not yet Phase 3-confirmed; predominantly female cohort (78%).
- Tolerability: grade A. Evidence base: GI + fatigue AEs, dose-dependent; similar to placebo at; slower escalation helped. Caveat: Strong — but the highest-efficacy (highest-dose) arms had more AEs; escalation protocol matters.
- Cardiometabolic risk factors: grade B. Evidence base: Improvements in waist, BP, lipids, glycemia, inflammation. Caveat: Consistent secondary benefits; Phase 2 level.
- Combination with tirzepatide: grade not graded. Evidence base: Trial ongoing (LY3841136 + tirzepatide). Caveat: No combination efficacy data yet.
- Safety: grade B. Evidence base: Phase 2 (263) + Phase 1. Caveat: No unexpected signals; investigational — Phase 3/long-term safety pending.
Legal status (EU): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). investigational; not approved. Phase 2 complete → Phase 3 enrolling (obesity). A well-capitalized Lilly clinical program, not a gray-market compound.
The amylin that hit incretin-level weight loss — and rewrote the class's market lesson. Eloralintide (Lilly) closes the register's amylin lineage on a high note, and it comes with a genuinely beautiful backstory. Three threads: (1) the amylin origin story — the whole class descends from a mysterious "junk" amyloid found clogging diabetics' pancreases for a century, which turned out to be a third islet hormone; (2) eloralintide is a selective amylin-receptor agonist that delivered up to ~20% weight loss in Phase 2 — matching tirzepatide-class numbers without an incretin mechanism; (3) landing weeks after Petrelintide (#69), it flips that entry's lesson: amylins can reach top-tier efficacy, so magnitude — not just tolerability — is now in play for the class. Straight to Phase 3.
Status note: Phase 3 obesity enrolling; eloralintide + tirzepatide combination and Phase 3 readouts are the catalysts — and the real test of whether ~20% holds at scale
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/eloralintide (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Epitalon (Epithalon)
title: Epitalon (Epithalon) — evidence, telomerase claims & status
text:
Epitalon (Epithalon) — peptide.
Also known as: Epithalon, Epithalone, AEDG, sequence Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly.
Epitalon is a synthetic tetrapeptide (AEDG) from Russian pineal-peptide research, heavily promoted for telomerase activation and longevity. In-vitro studies report telomerase upregulation and telomere elongation in human cells, and animal studies report lifespan effects — but the foundational evidence is dominated by a single research group, rigorous independent human evidence is lacking, and telomerase activity in cultured cells does not equal proven human longevity. It is not approved anywhere.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Telomerase upregulation / telomere elongation (in vitro): grade C. Evidence base: Human-cell studies — Khavinson 2003; a 2025 independent replication reported dose-dependent extension. Reported effect: Upregulation observed. Caveat: Foundational data dominated by one group; the quantitative pathway was only recently tested independently.
- Lifespan extension (animal): grade C. Evidence base: Drosophila, mice/rats — largely the Khavinson/Anisimov group. Reported effect: Reported increase. Caveat: Overwhelmingly single-group; independent replication limited.
- Human longevity / mortality / age-markers: grade D. Evidence base: Older Russian human studies. Reported effect: Claimed benefit. Caveat: Methodologically limited; not independently replicated to modern RCT standards.
- Circadian rhythm / sleep / melatonin: grade D. Evidence base: Older Russian studies. Reported effect: Suggestive. Caveat: Weak, non-replicated.
- Marketed "reverses aging / turns back the cellular clock": grade F. Evidence base: None rigorous. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Telomerase-in-cells ≠ proven human longevity; the extrapolation is unsupported.
- Safety & long-term: grade not graded. Evidence base: No modern human safety data. Reported effect: Unknown. Caveat: Telomerase activation carries a theoretical oncologic concern; long-term safety not established.
Legal status (EU): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). Not approved for human use; not eligible for magistral/officinal compounding (no Ph. Eur. monograph). Research-chemical sale does not authorise human use.
Legal status (US): Not FDA-approved (under review). On the PCAC agenda for 24 July 2026 (day two, with DSIP/Emideltide and Semax); removed from Category 2 in April 2026; FDA's pre-meeting briefing documents proposed not adding it to the 503A Bulks List. Advisory only; no final rule. Not approved.
Legal status (INT): Approved Russia/CIS · not FDA-approved. Not approved as a medicine in Western jurisdictions. (Khavinson's peptide "bioregulators" have a separate history in Russia; that is not Western approval.)
Research / reference — not for sale. No dosing, reconstitution, or administration is published (intentional). Neutral scientific reference only. This is a hype-heavy longevity compound — the honest story is the quality and independence of the evidence.
Status note: US review status evolving — re-check the July PCAC outcome and any Federal Register rule
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 2 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/epitalon (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Exosomes
title: Exosomes — evidence, uses & status
text:
Exosomes — blend.
Also known as: extracellular vesicles, exosome-like vesicles, EVs, MSC exosomes (stem-cell-derived), plant-derived exosomes, plant extracellular vesicles (PEVs), INCI: varies by source (e.g. plant/ferment exosome extracts).
Exosomes are tiny lipid-membrane vesicles (roughly 30–150 nm) that cells use to communicate — a real, serious piece of biology. Because the messaging system is universal, plant cells make them too, and cosmetic exosome products are almost always plant-derived (they also sit outside the human-tissue regulatory framework). But the honest position is that the whole exosome category in skincare is still early and not established: a 2026 review of 19 human studies found only short-term, unconfirmed associations, and a 2024 review concluded the regenerative-aesthetics field is not yet scientifically established. Plant cosmetic exosomes work through antioxidant delivery, soothing, and barrier support — they are NOT "regenerative" like the human/stem-cell exosomes used in clinical, often injected settings. Treat them as a promising supporting antioxidant carrier, not a proven results-driver. Full deep-dive: the exosomes explainer in the Journal.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Topical antioxidant / soothing / barrier support (plant-derived cosmetic exosomes): grade C. Evidence base: Mechanistically plausible — plant-derived exosomes carry antioxidant, vitamin, and anti-inflammatory cargo and are good nano-delivery vehicles — but the supporting data is mostly in-vitro (lab cells) or animal, with only small early human work.. Reported effect: Plausible, modest antioxidant and soothing/barrier support as a supporting cosmetic ingredient. Caveat: Most plant-exosome evidence is lab- or animal-based rather than human topical trials; benefits are modest and supportive, not transformative..
- Short-term skin-appearance benefits (exosomes broadly — hydration, elasticity, fine lines, pores, pigmentation): grade C. Evidence base: A 2026 systematic review pooling 19 human studies found short-term associations with improved hydration, elasticity, wrinkles, pores, and pigmentation.. Reported effect: Early, short-term associations reported. Caveat: Most studies were not randomised, methods were heterogeneous, and follow-up was limited — the review framed the effects as early and not yet confirmed. Much of this signal comes from injected or in-office MSC (stem-cell) exosomes in clinical settings, which does not transfer to a plant exosome in a cosmetic cream..
- "Regenerative" skin transformation from a topical (especially plant) exosome: grade D. Evidence base: No topical evidence; regenerative signalling is the claimed role of human/stem-cell exosomes used clinically (often injected).. Reported effect: Not supported for a plant/topical cosmetic. Caveat: Plant exosomes act via antioxidant delivery and soothing, not cellular regeneration. Any product implying a topical plant exosome will "regenerate" skin borrows from the human clinical/injected lane and overstates the evidence..
- Established anti-ageing category (the field as a whole): grade D. Evidence base: A 2024 PRISMA systematic review of regenerative aesthetics (covering exosomes alongside stem cells and PDRN).. Reported effect: Not established. Caveat: Despite some well-designed trials, the review found a prevalent gap in molecular and clinical evidence and concluded the field lacks the scientific rigour and regulatory compliance to be considered established..
- Injected / clinical (MSC) exosome therapy — for context, NOT cosmetic scope: grade not graded. Evidence base: Human/stem-cell exosomes are studied in clinical and in-office (often injected or post-procedure) settings, where more of the exosome evidence sits.. Reported effect: A separate, regulated, clinical lane — not a cosmetic topical. Caveat: As of 2025 the FDA had NOT approved any exosome product for therapeutic use outside clinical trials; human-derived exosomes trigger the HCT/P (human cells/tissues) framework, and injected use carries procedural risks. Out of scope for a cosmetic reference and not what a topical serum delivers..
Legal status (INT): Lawful cosmetic ingredient (plant-derived). Plant-derived exosomes fall outside the FDA HCT/P human-tissue framework (which governs only human-derived material), which is precisely why cosmetic exosome products are almost always plant-derived. Human-derived exosomes are NOT FDA-approved for therapeutic use outside clinical trials, trigger HCT/P regulation, and disease/treatment claims draw enforcement. This is regulatory context, not a health claim.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): delivers antioxidants that help defend the look of skin against daily stress; helps skin look and feel calm, comfortable, and hydrated; a lightweight delivery ingredient for a supported, healthy-looking complexion; supports the appearance of a resilient skin surface.
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: regenerates skin; repairs or renews tissue; reverses ageing; stimulates collagen; treats or heals any condition; FDA-approved; anti-inflammatory (as a drug claim).
Only plant-derived, cosmetic-appropriate exosomes are relevant to any sellable product, framed around appearance and comfort (antioxidant, soothing, barrier support) — never regeneration. The reference science below includes the human/clinical exosome context for completeness; it is a different, regulated lane and does not describe a cosmetic topical.
Honest note: The exosome category is early-evidence and heavily marketed. Cosmetic exosomes are plant-derived and act as antioxidant/soothing carriers, not regenerative agents; the "regeneration" story belongs to human/stem-cell exosomes in clinical, injected settings, which are unapproved outside trials and out of cosmetic scope.
Identity: Exosomes are extracellular vesicles — tiny, lipid-membrane-bound sacs, roughly 30–150 nanometres across — that cells release to communicate with one another. Inside, they carry a mixed cargo of signalling molecules: proteins, lipids, RNA, antioxidants, and peptides. The key fact most marketing skips is that this messaging system is universal: human cells, animal cells, and plant cells all release exosomes.

That universality is why "exosomes" in a cosmetic almost always means plant-derived exosomes (also called plant extracellular vesicles, or PEVs) — nano-sized, lipid-based particles from sources like strawberry, green tea, aloe, grape, ginseng, or centella. They can fuse with skin-cell membranes and deliver their cargo, which is the mechanistic basis for their cosmetic use. This entry is a short, graded reference; the full detective breakdown is in the Journal explainer, what the science actually says about exosomes.
Mechanism (as proposed): Plant-derived cosmetic exosomes are best understood as biologically active nano-carriers. Their lipid membrane lets them associate with the skin surface and deliver an antioxidant, vitamin, and anti-inflammatory cargo, which is the plausible basis for antioxidant defence, soothing, and barrier support. Their small size (especially under ~100 nm) aids penetration.

What they are not is "cell-regenerating." Regenerative signalling — prompting collagen and tissue repair — is the claimed role of human/stem-cell exosomes, which are a different payload used in clinical, often injected contexts. Plant exosomes carry a non-human cargo and support the skin's appearance rather than remodelling its structure. Two honest caveats frame the whole category: most plant exosome data is still in-vitro or animal rather than human topical trials; and the broader positive signals for exosomes come largely from injected or in-office human/MSC exosomes, which do not transfer to a plant exosome in a cream. The reviews are blunt — the field is promising but not yet established.
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/exosomes (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Follistatin (FST-344)
title: Follistatin (FST-344) — myostatin inhibition, what the evidence really shows & status
text:
Follistatin (FST-344) — protein.
Also known as: Follistatin, FST, FST-344 / FS344 (the "research-peptide" isoform), FST-315 (the circulating form), FST-288 (the tissue-bound form). INCI: none.
Follistatin is a natural myostatin inhibitor whose biology is spectacular — releasing the body's brake on muscle produces the "double-muscling" seen across species. But "follistatin, the injectable muscle drug" does not exist in any proven form: the dramatic gains are from transgenic animals and gene therapy, the human gene-therapy trials were modest and unapproved, the pharma myostatin-inhibitor programs mostly failed on function or safety, and the online "FST-344" is a large protein of dubious authenticity. Fascinating science; unproven and risky as a product. Banned in sport.
Overall evidence grade: D (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Muscle hypertrophy via myostatin inhibition (the biology): grade not graded. Evidence base: Transgenic + gene-therapy animals (20–40%, more in overexpression); Lee 2007. Reported effect: Dramatic muscle growth. Caveat: This is the biology in transgenic / gene-therapy models — not injected protein in humans.
- Injected follistatin protein → muscle in healthy humans (the marketed use): grade F. Evidence base: None. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: No human clinical evidence; the animal gains were transgenic / gene-therapy.
- Gene therapy for muscular dystrophy / IBM: grade D. Evidence base: Phase 1/2 (Mendell). Reported effect: Modest. Caveat: Well tolerated locally, but modest; never became an approved therapy.
- Broader myostatin-inhibitor drug class: grade not graded. Evidence base: Antibodies (MYO-029 etc.) + ACE-031. Reported effect: Largely failed. Caveat: Antibodies: modest/no functional benefit; ACE-031 terminated on vascular safety.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Preclinical + gene-therapy + mechanism. Reported effect: Real concerns. Caveat: Suppresses FSH/LH (reproductive); vascular risk (class); may lower bone density (GDF-11 block); muscle may outpace tendon; theoretical oncogenic concern; effects may not "cycle off"; gray-market product often mislabelled/contaminated; WADA-banned.
Legal status (INT): See note. - Everywhere — not approved for any human indication. Sold only as a "research" chemical — and because it's a large protein, gray-market "Follistatin-344" is frequently underdosed, mislabelled or contaminated.
- ⚠ Sport: WADA-prohibited — myostatin-inhibiting agents are explicitly banned (Section S4.4, Hormone and Metabolic Modulators). Do not touch it in tested sport.
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport: WADA-prohibited — myostatin-inhibiting agents are explicitly banned (Section S4.4, Hormone and Metabolic Modulators). Do not touch it in tested sport.
Reference entry — not sold here. The muscle world's holy grail — myostatin inhibition — and the honest reality behind the hype. Two things to know up front: it's a large protein, not a peptide, and the jaw-dropping numbers you've seen come from transgenic animals and gene therapy, not from an injected protein. No dosing published here.
Honest note: the myostatin story is real and thrilling — but the gap between "myostatin knockout doubles muscle in mice" and "inject this vial and grow" is enormous and unbridged. The compounds that tried to bridge it with rigorous trials mostly failed (function or safety), and what's sold online is a large, fragile protein you can't verify. Respect the biology; don't trust the product.
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 1 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/follistatin (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017)
title: Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017) — the HGF/MET Alzheimer's drug that failed: the debunk and the data-fraud backstory
text:
Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017) — small_molecule.
Also known as: Fosgonimeton, ATH-1017, NDX-1017 (earlier code).
Fosgonimeton (ATH-1017) is a small-molecule positive modulator of the HGF/MET system — the growth-factor pathway that's reduced in the Alzheimer's brain — developed by Athira Pharma as a once-daily injection for Alzheimer's. It matters to this register because it completes the story begun at Dihexa (#44): both target the same HGF/c-Met "brain growth factor" axis, but where Dihexa collapsed under retracted, fabricated data, fosgonimeton was taken all the way to a pivotal Phase 2/3 trial (LIFT-AD) — and honestly failed, missing its primary and key secondary endpoints (cognition and function no better than placebo). That's the rigorous debunk of the "enhance growth-factor signaling to treat neurodegeneration" hypothesis. The drama runs deeper: Athira's founding science was already tainted by a data-manipulation scandal (its CEO ousted over altered images) and an activist proxy fight, and after the failure the company clung to post-hoc subgroups while executives spun the miss as "encouraging." The honest read: a safe, well-tolerated drug for a compelling-sounding mechanism that, tested properly, did not help Alzheimer's patients — a clinical debunk wrapped in a research-integrity cautionary tale. Safety wasn't the problem; efficacy was.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Alzheimer's disease (the lead use): grade F. Evidence base: Phase 2/3 LIFT-AD: missed primary (GST) + key secondaries; ACT-AD Ph2 missed; earlier failures. Caveat: Tested and failed at pivotal scale — cognition/function no better than placebo (P≈0.70). Not "untested" — clearly negative.
- Parkinson's / Lewy-body dementia: grade F. Evidence base: SHAPE cut 75→28 patients, missed primary; "5-patient subgroup". Caveat: Underpowered, failed; subgroup grasping.
- Subgroup "signals" (APOE4, monotherapy, advanced disease): grade D. Evidence base: Post-hoc / prespecified subgroups after failed primaries. Caveat: Classic post-hoc rescue; hypothesis-generating at best, not evidence of efficacy.
- HGF/MET mechanism (as biology): grade C. Evidence base: Real: MET signaling diminished in AD hippocampus; neuroprotective in models. Caveat: The biology is legitimate; it didn't translate to clinical benefit — the whole point.
- Safety: grade B. Evidence base: Phase 1–3 exposure. Caveat: Consistently safe and well-tolerated — its failure was efficacy, not safety.
Legal status (US): Not approved. not approved for any indication; its lead Alzheimer's program failed Phase 2/3 (2024). Investigational; the pathway continues only in a separate ALS candidate (ATH-1105).
The rigorous clinical debunk of the "boost brain growth factors" idea — with a data-fraud scandal attached. Fosgonimeton finishes a story the register started at Dihexa (#44): it targets the same HGF/c-Met pathway, but unlike Dihexa (retracted for fabrication), it was actually taken to Phase 2/3 in Alzheimer's — and failed honestly. Two big stories: (1) the scientific debunk — the hypothesis that "positively modulating HGF/growth-factor signaling treats neurodegeneration" was tested rigorously and did not beat placebo; (2) the corporate drama — Athira's program was already shadowed by a data-manipulation scandal (its CEO ousted over altered images) and an activist proxy fight before the pivotal trial flopped. It's a small molecule, not a peptide.
Status note: Alzheimer's program failed/effectively discontinued; the HGF/MET pathway continues only via oral ATH-1105 in ALS — the remaining test of whether the mechanism helps any neurodegenerative disease
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/fosgonimeton (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: FOXO4-DRI
title: FOXO4-DRI — the senolytic "zombie-cell" peptide: the elegant p53 mechanism, the famous mice, and the human-data gap
text:
FOXO4-DRI — peptide.
Also known as: FOXO4-DRI, FOXO4 D-Retro-Inverso peptide, FOXO4-p53 disrupting peptide, "the senolytic peptide." (DRI = D-Retro-Inverso — see below.) INCI: none.
FOXO4-DRI is a synthetic senolytic peptide — it doesn't boost a pathway like NAD⁺; it selectively kills "zombie" (senescent) cells, the worn-out non-dividing cells that accumulate with age and secrete inflammation (the "SASP"). Its mechanism is one of the most elegant in this register: senescent cells survive by using the protein FOXO4 to trap p53 (which would otherwise order them to self-destruct); FOXO4-DRI competitively displaces FOXO4, freeing p53 to trigger apoptosis — but only in senescent cells, because healthy cells barely make FOXO4, so there's nothing to disrupt. The famous 2017 Cell study (de Keizer lab, Erasmus) showed naturally aged mice regrowing fur, recovering kidney function and stamina, with the balding-mouse-regrows-coat-in-10-days photo becoming an aging-science icon, and reported ~24.8% median-lifespan extension. The D-Retro-Inverso engineering (mirror-image amino acids, reversed) makes it protease-resistant and stable. The decisive caveat: it has never been tested in humans — no Phase 1, no safety data — and it works by systemically manipulating p53, the master tumor-suppressor. So: a beautiful, reproducible mechanism with striking mouse results and genuinely zero human evidence.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Senescent-cell clearance (senolysis): grade D. Evidence base: Landmark Cell 2017 + repeated validation (Nat Commun 2025, etc.): selective apoptosis of senescent cells (reported 30–70% reduction across tissues). Caveat: Genuinely elegant, reproducible preclinical senolysis — one of the few peptides to cross from in-vitro to in-vivo — but still animal/cell only.
- Healthspan / tissue-function restoration (aged mice): grade F. Evidence base: Cell 2017: restored fur, kidney function, physical fitness; ~24.8% median-lifespan extension. Caveat: Striking mouse result; no human data at all.
- Wound/keloid, testosterone (senescent Leydig cells), chemo-recovery: grade F. Evidence base: Comms Biol 2025; Aging 2020; Cell 2017 (doxorubicin recovery). Caveat: Interesting mechanism-extensions, all preclinical.
- Human efficacy or safety: grade F. Evidence base: None — no Phase 1 ever conducted. Caveat: The defining gap: human effects completely unknown.
- Mechanism / target engagement: grade B. Evidence base: Co-IP, fluorescence, 2025 structural work confirm selective p53-FOXO4 disruption. Caveat: The biology is well-demonstrated and beautiful; that's not the same as a safe human drug.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: 10-month mouse dosing "no obvious side effects". Caveat: Reassuring in mice — but it manipulates p53 systemically; long-term human oncologic/apoptotic consequences are unstudied.
Legal status (EU): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). not approved anywhere; investigational research peptide. Not a cosmetic. No human clinical data; no compounding pathway.
The "zombie-cell killer" — the most elegant senolytic mechanism in the register, and a famous mouse photo — with zero human data. This entry adds a third distinct longevity strategy alongside NAD⁺ (#36, "refuel the cell") and Epitalon (#35, "pineal clock"). Three threads: (1) it's a senolytic — it selectively kills senescent "zombie" cells rather than boosting a pathway; (2) its mechanism is genuinely beautiful — it frees p53 only in cells that sequester it, so healthy cells are untouched; (3) the famous 2017 Cell study showed aged, balding mice regrowing fur in 10 days and reported ~24.8% median-lifespan extension — but it's preclinical only, no human trials, and it manipulates the master tumor-suppressor p53.
Status note: status: preclinical; the decisive event would be a first-in-human Phase 1 — repeatedly anticipated, not yet done
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/foxo4-dri (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
title: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1) — evidence, uses & status
text:
GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1) — peptide.
Also known as: copper peptide, copper tripeptide, GHK-copper, glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine copper(II) complex, INCI: Copper Tripeptide-1.
GHK-Cu is a naturally occurring copper-binding tripeptide. As a topical cosmetic ingredient it has several small human studies showing improved appearance of photoaged skin (fine lines, firmness, density). As an injectable / systemic "research" compound it is studied mainly in vitro and in animals, with little human data and no approval. A well-documented limitation: GHK-Cu barely penetrates intact skin, so topical results depend heavily on formulation and delivery.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Topical: appearance of photoaged skin (fine lines, firmness, density): grade B. Evidence base: Small human RCTs — Leyden 2002 (n=71), eye-cream (n=41), Badenhorst 2016 (nano-carrier vs Matrixyl 3000). Reported effect: Measurable improvement. Caveat: Small samples; effect generally smaller than prescription retinoids; highly formulation-dependent.
- Collagen/elastin synthesis & balanced MMP/TIMP remodeling: grade B. Evidence base: In-vitro fibroblast studies (Maquart 1988; Pickart). Reported effect: Stimulates synthesis; orderly turnover. Caveat: In vitro; in-vivo relevance limited by skin penetration.
- Skin penetration of intact-skin serums: grade not graded. Evidence base: Permeation study (Li 2015). Reported effect: Near-zero through intact stratum corneum. Caveat: Key limitation; most benefit shown with enhanced delivery or penetration-optimised formulas.
- Post-procedure wound healing (e.g., after laser resurfacing): grade C. Evidence base: Small controlled trials + animal models. Reported effect: Faster healing / less irritation. Caveat: Medicinal context — not a cosmetic-product claim.
- Hair growth / density: grade D. Evidence base: Mechanistic + small studies. Reported effect: Suggestive. Caveat: Weak human evidence.
- Systemic / injectable "anti-aging / resets gene expression": grade F. Evidence base: In-vitro / bioinformatic (Connectivity Map, ~4,000 genes toward a "younger" profile). Reported effect: Broad gene modulation in vitro. Caveat: Bioinformatic/in vitro only; no human systemic-efficacy trials; "reverses aging" is not demonstrated.
- Safety (topical): grade not graded. Evidence base: Widely used cosmetic; small trials. Reported effect: Well tolerated. Caveat: Topical generally well tolerated (occasional mild redness); systemic/injectable human safety not established.
Legal status (INT): See note. Topical (INCI Copper Tripeptide-1) — a lawful cosmetic ingredient in the EU (Regulation (EC) 1223/2009) and widely used globally; this is the form sold. Claims are constrained by cosmetic regulation — see the cosmetic_claims block.

Injectable / systemic — Not approved anywhere as a medicine. EU: not eligible for magistral / officinal compounding (no Ph. Eur. monograph exists). US: on 15 April 2026 the FDA removed injectable GHK-Cu from the Category 2 bulks list (previously restricting 503A compounding since 2023–2024) but did NOT add it to Category 1 — creating an interim regulatory posture. A PCAC (Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee) review of both injectable and non-injectable GHK-Cu is scheduled before end-February 2027; some 503A compounding pharmacies have resumed preparation on the basis of the Category 2 removal, others await affirmative Category 1 authorization. The topical / non- injectable route sits on Category 1 pending the same PCAC review. Recheck the early-2027 PCAC outcome before any US injectable references are updated.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): for the appearance of firmer, smoother-looking skin; helps improve the look of fine lines; for a more even, radiant-looking complexion; antioxidant.
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: stimulates collagen synthesis; wound healing; heals; repairs skin; increases skin density/thickness; appearance of denser-looking skin; reverses aging; resets gene expression; treats…; hair growth; increases hair density.
Dual-form compound. The topical form (INCI Copper Tripeptide-1) is a lawful cosmetic ingredient — this is the sellable serum. The injectable / systemic form is a research compound, not approved. Reference science below is neutral; the sellable product uses cosmetic (appearance/feel) claims only — see the cosmetic_claims block above.
Status note: US injectable status: interim — Category 2 removed 15 April 2026, PCAC review scheduled before end-February 2027; recheck after outcome
Identity: a tripeptide (3 amino acids: glycine, L-histidine, L-lysine, < 40 aa → peptide) coordinated with a copper(II) ion. Occurs naturally in human plasma, saliva and urine; plasma levels decline ~60% between ages ~20 and ~60. First described by Loren Pickart (1973).
Mechanism (as proposed): a copper-binding tripeptide that, in vitro, stimulates fibroblast collagen/elastin synthesis (Maquart 1988), modulates MMP/TIMP balance (orderly remodeling), acts as an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory (lowers IL-6/TNF-α), and is pro-angiogenic (VEGF); bioinformatic analyses report broad gene-expression modulation. Most of this is in vitro; in-vivo topical relevance is constrained by poor skin penetration.
Evidence provenance: 8 cited sources, 2 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/ghk-cu (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: GHRP-2 (Pralmorelin)
title: GHRP-2 (pralmorelin) — evidence, the Japan diagnostic approval & status
text:
GHRP-2 (Pralmorelin) — peptide.
Also known as: GHRP-2, Growth Hormone Releasing Peptide-2, INN pralmorelin, developer codes KP-102 / GPA-748, Japanese product GHRP Kaken 100, sequence D-Ala-D-2-Nal-Ala-Trp-D-Phe-Lys-NH₂ (MW ~818 Da). INCI: none.
GHRP-2 (pralmorelin) is a ghrelin-receptor agonist and the only GHRP with a regulatory approval — in Japan, as a diagnostic agent for GH deficiency. It reliably raises GH (the most potent GHRP per microgram) with intermediate selectivity — moderate cortisol/prolactin and appetite effects (more than Ipamorelin, less than GHRP-6). But the approval is diagnostic-only; the popular anti-aging / body-composition use is untested in humans. It is banned in sport.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- GH release (diagnostic): grade B. Evidence base: Approved diagnostic (Japan) + ~4 human trials (~170). Reported effect: Consistent dose-dependent GH elevation (peak 15–30 min). Caveat: Real but narrow; diagnostic-focused; single company.
- GH-deficiency diagnostic testing (approved, Japan): grade B. Evidence base: Kaken trials; accuracy competitive with the insulin-tolerance test. Reported effect: Reliable provocative test. Caveat: Approved diagnostic use only.
- Anti-aging / body composition (the popular use): grade F. Evidence base: None in humans. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Untested for these goals.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Diagnostic use + PK data. Reported effect: Characterised (short-term). Caveat: Raises cortisol/prolactin (moderate) + appetite; long-term unknown; WADA-banned.
Legal status (US): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). not FDA-approved; has not been through the FDA drug-approval process (503A Category-2 / gray-market for off-label use).
Legal status (EU): Not approved. not approved.
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — Section S2.2 (GH secretagogues).
Reference entry — not sold here. Notable as the only GHRP with a regulatory approval anywhere — but that approval is a diagnostic one (Japan), not a therapy. No dosing published here.
Evidence provenance: 2 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/ghrp-2 (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: GHRP-6
title: GHRP-6 — evidence, the ghrelin-discovery story & status
text:
GHRP-6 — peptide.
Also known as: GHRP-6, Growth Hormone Releasing Peptide-6, sequence His-D-Trp-Ala-Trp-D-Phe-Lys-NH₂. INCI: none.
GHRP-6 is the original growth-hormone-releasing peptide (Bowers, 1984) — the compound from which the entire GH-secretagogue class descends, and whose study led to the discovery of the ghrelin receptor (1996) and ghrelin itself (1999). It reliably raises GH but is non-selective, producing the strongest appetite stimulation of any GHRP plus modest cortisol/prolactin. It is not approved anywhere, and the popular anti-aging / muscle use is untested in humans. Banned in sport.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- GH release: grade B. Evidence base: Extensive characterisation (Bowers 1984 onward) + human PK. Reported effect: Strong, dose-dependent GH pulse. Caveat: Real, foundational pharmacology.
- Appetite stimulation (cachexia / wasting research): grade C. Evidence base: Preclinical + Phase 1 observations. Reported effect: Strongest of any GHRP. Caveat: A feature for cachexia, a drawback for GH/fat-loss goals; preclinical as a therapy.
- Anti-aging / body composition / muscle (the popular use): grade F. Evidence base: None in humans. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Untested for these goals.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Research + PK data. Reported effect: Characterised (short-term). Caveat: Strong hunger; raises cortisol (~15–20% transient) and prolactin; long-term unknown; WADA-banned.
Legal status (INT): See note. - Everywhere — not approved for human therapeutic use; a research compound (503A Category-2 / gray-market). (Unlike GHRP-2, GHRP-6 has no approval anywhere.)
- ⚠ Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — Section S2.2 (GH secretagogues).
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — Section S2.2 (GH secretagogues).
Reference entry — not sold here. Historically the most important peptide in this class: the first GHRP, and the compound whose study led to the discovery of ghrelin. Not approved anywhere; the marketed anti-aging use is untested in humans. No dosing published here.
Evidence provenance: 3 cited sources, 1 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/ghrp-6 (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Glutathione (GSH)
title: Glutathione — the "master antioxidant" and Asia's skin-whitening story: mechanism, evidence, and the IV-safety warnings
text:
Glutathione (GSH) — peptide.
Also known as: Glutathione, GSH (reduced form), L-glutathione, γ-glutamyl-cysteinyl-glycine.
Glutathione (GSH) is a tripeptide that serves as the body's master antioxidant — genuinely essential intracellular chemistry. Its fame, though, comes from a striking human phenomenon: across much of Asia it became the engine of a skin-whitening movement, sold as capsules and especially intravenous "gluta drips" promising lighter skin "from within," to the point that the Philippine FDA had to issue public warnings. The lightening mechanism is real in principle — glutathione inhibits tyrosinase and nudges pigment synthesis from dark eumelanin toward lighter pheomelanin — but the clinical evidence is mixed and route-dependent: oral glutathione has low bioavailability and modest, inconsistent effects; topical works only locally; and the wildly popular IV route has essentially no efficacy evidence and is unapproved, with health authorities flagging risks including liver/kidney/thyroid effects, severe skin reactions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome), infection from non-sterile injection, and a theoretical long-term skin-cancer concern (less melanin means less UV protection). So the honest picture: a truly important antioxidant and a huge cultural phenomenon — but weak, mostly-unproven evidence for the skin-lightening use it's sold for, and a genuinely risky most-popular route. Notably, it's the pigment-lightening opposite of the register's tanning peptides (Melanotan #32 / Afamelanotide #55).
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Endogenous antioxidant / detox role: grade A. Evidence base: Foundational, non-controversial cell biology. Caveat: Grades GSH's natural role — NOT proof that supplementation delivers cosmetic benefits.
- Oral glutathione — skin lightening: grade C. Evidence base: Some RCT/clinical data (often with vitamin C); modest, inconsistent; low bioavailability. Caveat: Mixed results; bioavailability doubts; effect (if any) is subtle and reversible.
- Topical glutathione — pigmentation: grade C. Evidence base: Local depigmentation data. Caveat: Effect limited to application site; not systemic.
- IV glutathione — skin lightening (the most popular use): grade F. Evidence base: No published trials proving efficacy; unapproved. Caveat: Popular but essentially unproven; Philippine FDA: no evidence, no dosing standard.
- Anti-aging / wrinkles / elasticity: grade D. Evidence base: One oral study noted possible wrinkle/elasticity benefit + good tolerance. Caveat: Preliminary; not established.
- Safety — oral/topical: grade B. Evidence base: GRAS; generally well-tolerated. Caveat: The reassuring part: oral/topical is broadly safe.
- Safety — IV (the decisive warning): grade not graded. Evidence base: Health-authority advisories. Caveat: Renal/hepatic/thyroid dysfunction, nervous-system effects, Stevens-Johnson syndrome; infection risk (HIV/hepatitis) if non-sterile; IV+vitC → kidney-stone risk; theoretical skin-cancer risk (less eumelanin = less UV protection). Narrow effective-vs-toxic window.
Legal status (INT): See note. - Oral supplement — widely legal: GRAS/OTC dietary-supplement status in the US, Philippines, Japan, India and elsewhere; no prescription needed.
- Topical — used as a cosmetic ingredient (effect limited to the application site).
- ⚠ Injectable / IV — not FDA-approved for skin lightening anywhere; off-label; the Philippine FDA and dermatological societies warn against it; in the US it is legal only if prescribed by a professional and compounded by a licensed pharmacy. Online/unlicensed injectable glutathione is exactly what authorities warn against.
- Gray-market note: heavily sold (especially IV) for whitening/anti-aging. Oral/topical glutathione is a legal lane; injectable skin-lightening is not, and should never be sold.
The "master antioxidant" — and the molecule behind Asia's skin-whitening phenomenon. Glutathione's human story is one of the most vivid in the register: a critical cellular antioxidant that became a massive cosmetic movement for lighter skin — sold as IV "gluta drips" across the Philippines, Thailand, India and beyond — despite thin efficacy evidence and real safety warnings. Three threads: (1) endogenously it's a genuinely essential master antioxidant; (2) its fame rests on skin-lightening via tyrosinase inhibition — a real mechanism but weak, route-dependent clinical proof; (3) the popular IV route is unapproved, unproven for lightening, and flagged by health authorities for serious harms. Big real biology, big cultural phenomenon, small honest evidence for the thing it's sold for.
Status note: watch: better-bioavailability oral forms (liposomal/acetyl) with real RCTs; IV skin-lightening remains unapproved and risk-flagged
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 2 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/glutathione (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Hexarelin (Examorelin)
title: Hexarelin (examorelin) — evidence, the CD36 cardiac story & status
text:
Hexarelin (Examorelin) — peptide.
Also known as: Hexarelin, INN examorelin, developer code EP 23905.
Hexarelin (examorelin) is the most potent classic GHRP and the most distinctive — uniquely, alongside the ghrelin receptor it binds the cardiac CD36 receptor, producing direct cardioprotective effects independent of GH (documented even in GH-deficient models, and abolished in CD36-knockouts). It reached Phase 2 for both GH deficiency and heart failure but was never approved. The cardiac biology is genuinely interesting and legitimate — but it is preclinical/early, and the popular anti-aging use is untested in humans. Banned in sport.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- GH release: grade B. Evidence base: Most potent GHRP (~10× GHRP-6); human PK. Reported effect: Strong, sustained GH pulse. Caveat: Real, potent pharmacology; GH response desensitises with continuous use.
- Cardioprotection (CD36, GH-independent): grade C. Evidence base: Preclinical (infarct ↓40–60%, LV function ↑, anti-fibrotic; CD36-KO abolishes it) + incomplete Phase 2. Reported effect: Genuine, mechanistically distinct. Caveat: Preclinical / early; Phase 2 didn't complete; not an established therapy.
- Metabolic / "fat-burning" (CD36 / adipocyte): grade C. Evidence base: Preclinical (Rodrigue-Way 2007). Reported effect: Mitochondrial biogenesis. Caveat: Preclinical.
- Anti-aging / body composition (the popular use): grade F. Evidence base: None in humans. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Untested for these goals.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Research + PK data. Reported effect: Characterised (short-term). Caveat: Raises cortisol/prolactin; GH desensitisation; WADA-banned.
Legal status (INT): See note. - Everywhere — not approved for human therapeutic use (Phase 2 reached, never marketed). Research compound. Not FDA-approved, and reportedly not on the FDA Category-2 list (a slightly different position from GHRP-2/6).
- ⚠ Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — Section S2.2 (GH secretagogues).
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — Section S2.2 (GH secretagogues).
Reference entry — not sold here. The most potent of the classic GHRPs and the most pharmacologically distinctive — the only one that also acts on a cardiac receptor, with effects independent of growth hormone. No dosing published here.
Evidence provenance: 3 cited sources, 1 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/hexarelin (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Humanin (HN / HNG / S14G-Humanin)
title: Humanin — the founding mitochondrial peptide: rich biology, biomarker status, no human trials
text:
Humanin (HN / HNG / S14G-Humanin) — peptide.
Also known as: Humanin, HN, HNG / S14G-Humanin / [Gly14]-Humanin (the potent synthetic analog), HNGF6A (a further-modified metabolic analog), rattin (the rat ortholog). INCI: none.
Humanin is the founding mitochondrial-derived peptide — a 24-amino-acid signal encoded in mitochondrial DNA, discovered in 2001 from an Alzheimer's-resistant neuron. Its molecular biology is unusually well-mapped: it binds a specific cell-surface receptor complex to switch on survival signalling, directly blocks the cell-death protein BAX, and modulates IGF-1 signalling — engaging two of the most conserved longevity pathways (mitochondrial health and IGF-1/insulin) at once. In humans, circulating humanin falls with age, is lower in Alzheimer's and coronary disease, and is higher in centenarians and their children — making it a compelling longevity biomarker. But that is the ceiling of the human evidence: it is correlational and observational. There is no completed interventional human trial of administering humanin for anything, no established human pharmacokinetics, and no dose-ranging data — and most of the striking preclinical work used the HNG analog (~1000× more potent), not the native peptide typically sold. It is an intriguing hypothesis, not a demonstrated therapy.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Longevity biomarker (levels correlate with lifespan/health): grade C. Evidence base: Multiple human observational/epidemiological datasets (USC, Barzilai): humanin ↓ with age, ↓ in AD/CAD, ↑ in centenarians + their offspring; correlates across mammalian species. Caveat: This is the strongest human evidence — but it's a biomarker correlation, not proof that giving humanin helps. Human association studies are even called "equivocal" in some reviews. Biomarker ≠ drug.
- Neuroprotection / Alzheimer's (the discovery context): grade F. Evidence base: Rich preclinical: blocks amyloid-beta toxicity, prevents tau hyperphosphorylation, anti-apoptotic in neuron/animal models (mostly HNG). Caveat: Powerful mechanism, zero completed human trials; no RCT shows exogenous humanin prevents or treats AD. Founding story ≠ clinical proof.
- Insulin sensitivity / metabolic: grade F. Evidence base: Rodent clamp studies (Muzumdar 2009 — central STAT3-mediated; HNGF6A analog lowered glucose in diabetic rats). Caveat: Preclinical rodent only, small n; CNS-mediated mechanism; not shown in humans.
- Cardioprotection (ischemia, age-related fibrosis): grade F. Evidence base: Animal: ischemia-reperfusion protection; 14-month HNG dosing prevented age-related myocardial fibrosis in aged mice. Caveat: Animal-only; native-vs-HNG gap applies.
- "Anti-aging / healthspan" (the popular use): grade F. Evidence base: Transgenic worms + multiple mammalian species (levels track lifespan); HNG improved healthspan markers, lowered inflammation in mice. Caveat: The headline longevity-therapeutic claim is animal + biomarker, not human-interventional. This is what most buyers want — and it's unproven in people.
- Safety (exogenous administration in humans): grade not graded. Evidence base: No completed human trials → no human safety dataset. Caveat: Endogenous peptide (present in everyone), which is reassuring mechanistically, but injected exogenous safety/PK is simply not characterised in humans.
Legal status (EU): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). not approved anywhere for any indication; not a cosmetic; investigational research peptide only. No EMA/FDA marketing authorisation, no compounding pathway.
The mitochondrial trio's third face — and the register's clearest "gorgeous mechanism, zero human trials" case. Three honest points define this entry: (1) it is the founding member of the mitochondrial-derived peptide (MDP) family — the same class as MOTS-c (#10) — with an unusually rich, well-mapped molecular biology; (2) it is a genuine longevity biomarker (higher in centenarians and their children) — but a biomarker is not a drug, and there is not one completed human trial of giving it; (3) almost all the impressive preclinical data uses the HNG analog (~1000× more potent), not the native "Humanin" sold in research channels — so even the animal data doesn't map cleanly onto the product.
Status note: watch for any first-in-human interventional trial — that would change the grades materially
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 1 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/humanin (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate)
title: Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate) — evidence, uses & status
text:
Hyaluronic Acid (Sodium Hyaluronate) — polysaccharide.
Also known as: HA, sodium hyaluronate, hyaluronan, low molecular weight HA (LMW-HA), high molecular weight HA (HMW-HA), INCI: Hyaluronic Acid, INCI: Sodium Hyaluronate.
Hyaluronic acid (HA) is a naturally occurring polysaccharide that binds up to a thousand times its weight in water. As a topical cosmetic ingredient, HA reliably improves skin hydration (Grade A) and — at lower molecular weights — the appearance of fine lines and elasticity (Grade B). Higher MW forms sit on the surface; lower MW forms penetrate deeper. Effect is primarily humectant and surface-focused, not regenerative; HA does not build collagen or replace retinol, but it partners well with almost every other active. Injectable crosslinked HA is a separate medical-device category and outside the cosmetic scope.
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Topical: skin hydration (all molecular weights): grade A. Evidence base: Multiple placebo-controlled trials at 0.1% across MW range (50–2000 kDa); 2024 JCAD multi-weight formulation study; consistent effect across MW. Reported effect: Measurable immediate and sustained increase in skin hydration (corneometry); onset within 30 minutes. Caveat: Effect is humectant and surface-focused; depth depends on molecular weight and formulation.
- Fine lines and wrinkle appearance (LMW-HA 50–130 kDa): grade B. Evidence base: Pavicic 2011 RCT (n=76, periocular, 60 days, 5 MW compared); LMW-HA showed significant wrinkle depth reduction. Reported effect: Visible reduction of wrinkle depth after 4–8 weeks with low-molecular- weight formulations. Caveat: Effect smaller than retinoids or injectables; multi-weight formulations combine surface hydration with deeper penetration.
- Skin elasticity and appearance of firmness: grade B. Evidence base: Pavicic 2011; multi-weight formulation trials. Reported effect: Measurable improvement over 30–60 days. Caveat: Modest effect; depends on formulation, MW, and delivery system.
- Barrier function / TEWL reduction: grade B. Evidence base: Multiple controlled trials; multi-weight formulation studies. Reported effect: Reduction in transepidermal water loss. Caveat: Effect modest; primarily via humectant film and superficial hydration.
- Oral supplementation for skin hydration and wrinkles: grade B. Evidence base: 2025 meta-analysis of 7 RCTs; Nature Scientific Reports 2025 RCT n=150 with 1.8 MDa sodium hyaluronate. Reported effect: Statistically significant improvement in hydration, elasticity, and wrinkle depth. Caveat: Outside cosmetic topical scope; not a claim for a topical serum; effect sizes modest.
- Injectable dermal fillers (crosslinked HA, e.g. Restylane, Juvederm, Profhilo): grade A. Evidence base: Decades of clinical use; multiple RCTs and systematic reviews. Reported effect: Immediate volume restoration; effect lasts months. Caveat: Medical device territory, not cosmetic; requires practitioner and falls outside 1223/2009 cosmetic scope.
- Safety (topical): grade not graded. Evidence base: Decades of widespread cosmetic use across all molecular weights. Reported effect: Very well tolerated. Caveat: Occasional dryness in low-humidity environments if used without occlusive layer (humectant can pull water outward when air is dry).
Legal status (INT): Lawful cosmetic ingredient. Topical Hyaluronic Acid and Sodium Hyaluronate are lawful cosmetic ingredients globally, including EU (Regulation (EC) 1223/2009). CosIng- listed. No concentration ceiling. Injectable crosslinked HA (dermal fillers) are medical devices under separate regulation (EU MDR 2017/745) and are not sold as cosmetics.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): for the appearance of hydrated, plumper-looking skin; helps improve the look of dryness; supports a smooth, comfortable-feeling skin surface; for the appearance of a more supple, refreshed-looking complexion.
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: treats dry skin; repairs the skin barrier; restores dermal volume; builds collagen; reverses aging; fills wrinkles from within; regenerates skin.
Hyaluronic Acid and Sodium Hyaluronate are lawful cosmetic ingredients worldwide. Vallydia uses multi-molecular-weight HA in cosmetic serums with appearance-only claims. Reference science below includes topical cosmetic studies, oral supplementation trials, and injectable filler trials; only the topical cosmetic use is relevant to the sellable product.
Identity: a large, negatively-charged glycosaminoglycan (polysaccharide) composed of repeating disaccharide units of D-glucuronic acid and N-acetyl-D-glucosamine. Occurs naturally throughout the body, most abundantly in skin, joints, and eyes. In skin, HA is a major component of the extracellular matrix and dermal ground substance, holding water and maintaining tissue turgor. Endogenous HA declines with age. Used topically as sodium hyaluronate (the sodium salt, more stable in cosmetic formulations) in molecular weights ranging from ~10 kDa (very-low, deeply penetrating) through 2,000+ kDa (very-high, surface-forming).
Mechanism (as proposed): a humectant that binds water and holds it near the skin surface. High-molecular-weight HA forms a film on the stratum corneum, reducing transepidermal water loss and giving a smooth, plumped surface. Low-molecular-weight HA can penetrate into the upper epidermis, delivering hydration deeper and — per multi-MW clinical trials — reducing the appearance of fine lines. Very-low-MW HA fragments may signal to skin cells, though this is more relevant in wound-healing contexts than topical cosmetic use. HA is not enzymatically active on collagen or elastin — the appearance benefits come from hydration and surface effects, not from remodeling the extracellular matrix.
Evidence provenance: 6 cited sources, 3 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.

name: IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1)
title: IGF-1 LR3 — hyperplasia, what the evidence really shows & status
text:
IGF-1 LR3 (Long R3 IGF-1) — protein.
Also known as: IGF-1 LR3, Long R3 IGF-1, Long Arg3 IGF-1, LR3-IGF-1, a synthetic analog of Insulin-like Growth Factor 1. INCI: none.
IGF-1 LR3 is a long-acting engineered version of IGF-1, prized in the muscle community for one genuinely special property — hyperplasia, the formation of new muscle fibres via satellite cells, which steroids cannot do. But the honest reality is stark: it was created as a cell-culture reagent, no human trial has ever tested it for muscle or anything else, the hyperplasia effect in humans is debated, and its safety profile — hypoglycemia, tumour-promotion risk, organ overgrowth — is serious. Thrilling mechanism; unproven and genuinely risky product. Banned in sport.
Overall evidence grade: D (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Muscle hypertrophy + hyperplasia via IGF-1R (the biology): grade not graded. Evidence base: Cell culture + animal models. Reported effect: Anabolic; satellite-cell activation; new fibres. Caveat: The biology, in a dish and in animals — not injected LR3 in humans.
- IGF-1 LR3 for muscle building in humans (the marketed use): grade F. Evidence base: None. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Zero human trials for any endpoint; it's a cell-culture reagent.
- Hyperplasia in humans specifically: grade D. Evidence base: Debated. Reported effect: Uncertain. Caveat: Established in vitro/animal; human extent at realistic doses is disputed.
- Native IGF-1 (mecasermin) — pediatric IGF-1 deficiency: grade not graded. Evidence base: FDA/EMA-approved. Reported effect: Improves growth velocity. Caveat: Native form, rare pediatric indication — not LR3, not muscle.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Mecasermin record + IGF-1R pharmacology. Reported effect: Serious concerns. Caveat: Hypoglycemia (primary acute risk; with insulin → hospitalisations reported); tumour promotion — LR3 is engineered to evade the IGFBP system that normally reins IGF-1 in, an unstudied long-term oncogenic concern; acromegaly-like organ growth; insulin resistance; gray-market misfolding/endotoxin; WADA-banned.
Legal status (INT): See note. - Everywhere — not approved for muscle, performance or anti-aging. Native IGF-1 (mecasermin / Increlex) is FDA- and EMA-approved for pediatric severe primary IGF-1 deficiency only; IGF-1 LR3 is not separately authorised for anything therapeutic — it is a research / cell-culture reagent sold gray-market for bodybuilding.
- ⚠ Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — Section S2 (Peptide Hormones, Growth Factors); validated LongR3-IGF-I detection assays exist. Banned in and out of competition.
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — Section S2 (Peptide Hormones, Growth Factors); validated LongR3-IGF-I detection assays exist (Mongongu 2020). Banned in and out of competition.
Reference entry — not sold here. The muscle world's hyperplasia dream — building new muscle fibres, not just bigger ones — and the honest reality behind it. Three things up front: it's a protein, not a peptide; there are zero human trials of it for muscle; and the safety profile is genuinely serious. No dosing published here.
Honest note: the hyperplasia idea is real and genuinely exciting — but it's proven in a Petri dish and lab animals, and the exact molecule you'd buy was designed to grow cells in that dish, not muscle in you. Zero human trials, a hypoglycemia risk that has put people in hospital, and — because it's built to evade the very system that normally keeps IGF-1 in check — a cancer concern that is essentially unstudied over the long term. Respect the mechanism; don't trust the vial.
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/igf-1-lr3 (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Ipamorelin
title: Ipamorelin — evidence, development history & status
text:
Ipamorelin — peptide.
Also known as: Ipamorelin, Novo Nordisk code NNC 26-0161, CAS 170851-70-4, sequence Aib-His-D-2-Nal-D-Phe-Lys-NH₂. INCI: none.
Ipamorelin is a selective ghrelin-receptor agonist that raises GH in a clean pulse (without the cortisol/prolactin rise of older GHRPs) — a real and well-characterised pharmacology. But its only rigorously tested indication (postoperative ileus) failed its primary endpoint, it is not approved anywhere, and its popular anti-aging / fat-loss / muscle use has never been tested in humans. Banned in sport.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Selective GH release (no cortisol/prolactin) in humans: grade B. Evidence base: Raun 1998 + human PK studies. Reported effect: Clean, selective GH pulse. Caveat: Selectivity + GH release are genuine; this is not the same as clinical benefit.
- Clinical efficacy (postoperative ileus — the only tested indication): grade D. Evidence base: Phase 2 RCT (Beck 2014, n=87). Reported effect: Failed primary endpoint. Caveat: The single rigorous human trial did not work.
- Anti-aging / fat loss / muscle (the popular use): grade F. Evidence base: None in humans. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Never tested in humans for these goals — "selectivity is real, efficacy for body composition is untested".
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Well tolerated in the POI trials; no long-term data. Reported effect: Generally tolerated. Caveat: No long-term human data; GH/IGF-1 elevation carries theoretical risks; WADA-banned.
Legal status (EU): Not approved. Not approved; not eligible for magistral/officinal compounding.
Legal status (US): Not FDA-approved (503A Cat. 2). Placed in 503A Category 2 (2023); status in flux amid the 2026 reclassification, but not among the seven peptides in the specific July 23–24 2026 PCAC review; not FDA-approved.
Legal status (INT): Not approved. Not approved.
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — GH secretagogues / GHRPs fall under Section S2.2.
Research / reference — not for sale. No dosing, reconstitution, or administration is published (intentional). Neutral scientific reference only.
Evidence provenance: 3 cited sources, 2 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/ipamorelin (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Kisspeptin-10 (KP-10)
title: Kisspeptin-10 — the reproductive master-switch peptide: the chocolate name, the cancer origin, and the real trials
text:
Kisspeptin-10 (KP-10) — peptide.
Also known as: Kisspeptin-10, KP-10, metastin (the parent's original name).
Kisspeptin-10 is the minimal active fragment of kisspeptin — the hypothalamic peptide that sits at the top of the reproductive hormone axis and acts as its master switch. It works upstream: activating KISS1R on GnRH neurons, which drives pulsatile GnRH → LH/FSH → testosterone/estradiol. Its story is unusually rich: the gene was named after Hershey's Kisses chocolate, was discovered in cancer research (as the metastasis suppressor "metastin") before its reproductive role was known, and proved essential when people lacking it were found not to undergo puberty. Crucially — and unlike most compounds here — kisspeptin has real, current clinical science behind it: Imperial College London teams have run trials in hypogonadism, IVF triggering, hypothalamic amenorrhea, and hypoactive sexual desire disorder, where randomized placebo-controlled studies showed it modulates the brain's arousal/"attraction" circuits and reduces sexual aversion. The honest caveats: it's still research-only (not approved), its strongest data are in specific clinical settings (not the bodybuilding "testosterone restart" use it's often sold for), and it has a very short half-life. Bottom line: a genuinely important hormone with real translational momentum — promising and well-studied, but not yet an approved therapy.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Stimulating the reproductive axis (LH/FSH release): grade B. Evidence base: Robust, repeatedly demonstrated in humans (IV kisspeptin → rapid LH/FSH rise); genetics confirm necessity. Caveat: Well-established physiology in controlled settings; short-acting; response shows sexual dimorphism (differs by sex and menstrual-cycle phase).
- IVF / egg-maturation triggering: grade B. Evidence base: Clinical trials: kisspeptin can trigger oocyte maturation with lower OHSS risk than standard hCG. Caveat: Promising, actively developed; not yet a standard/approved trigger.
- Hypoactive sexual desire disorder (HSDD) — arousal/attraction: grade C. Evidence base: 2 randomized placebo-controlled trials (JAMA Netw Open 2022/2023, 32 women + 32 men): modulated sexual-processing brain networks, ↑"sexy" ratings, ↓aversion. Caveat: Real RCT signal on brain processing + self-report — but early, small, mechanistic endpoints; not an approved HSDD drug.
- Idiopathic hypogonadotropic hypogonadism / hypothalamic amenorrhea: grade C. Evidence base: Mechanistic + clinical studies restoring axis activity. Caveat: Coherent, promising; still investigational.
- "PCT / natural-testosterone restart" (the bodybuilding use): grade F. Evidence base: Extrapolated from LH/FSH stimulation. Caveat: Not clinically established for this purpose; short half-life and pulsatility make casual self-use dubious.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Controlled-setting trials. Caveat: Reported well-tolerated with no significant adverse events in trials; but off-protocol self-dosing of an upstream reproductive hormone is unstudied; product quality unverified.
Legal status (EU): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). research use only; not approved by FDA or EMA for any therapeutic use. But — unlike most gray-market peptides — it has an active, legitimate clinical-trial pipeline (Phase 1/2), so its "unproven" status is "not yet approved, actively being developed," not "fringe."
The reproductive "master switch" — with real modern clinical science and three great backstories. This is one of the register's stronger entries: a genuine upstream controller of human reproduction, backed by actual randomized trials (Imperial College London), yet still research-only. Three threads of intrigue: (1) it's named after Hershey's Kisses chocolate; (2) it was discovered in cancer research (as a metastasis suppressor) before anyone knew it ran reproduction; (3) recent placebo-controlled trials show it modulates the "attraction" and desire circuits of the brain — a real, published sexual-desire signal, not gray-market folklore.
Status note: active clinical pipeline — watch for Phase 2/3 progress in IVF triggering and HSDD; approval would move several grades
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/kisspeptin-10 (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Kojic Acid
title: Kojic Acid — evidence, uses & status
text:
Kojic Acid — small_molecule.
Also known as: KA, 5-hydroxy-2-(hydroxymethyl)-4H-pyran-4-one, INCI: Kojic Acid.
Kojic acid (KA) is a fungal fermentation product and a long-established tyrosinase inhibitor — it works by chelating the copper the enzyme needs. Its mechanism is strong (among the most consistent tyrosinase inhibitors in comparative lab work) and there is some standalone clinical signal: a 2013 RCT found 1% kojic acid alone reduced melasma severity. But the register grade is held at C because the caveats stack higher than for any other brightener here: most efficacy data comes from 2–4%, above the EU's 1% cap (Regulation 2024/996), so compliant products are milder than the studies; it is the brightener most associated with allergic contact dermatitis; it is chemically unstable and photosensitising; and it is almost always used in combination, so isolated evidence at a compliant strength is limited. A real ingredient with a real mechanism — honestly the most caveated of the pigment set.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Hyperpigmentation / melasma (appearance, usually in combination): grade C. Evidence base: Deo 2013 randomized study — 1% kojic acid alone reduced melasma severity, with the combination against hydroquinone stronger. Reported effect: Gradual fading of melasma-related pigmentation. Caveat: Most efficacy data comes from 2–4%, above the EU 1% cap; often used in combination.
- PIH (appearance, in combination serums): grade C. Evidence base: Combination-serum evidence (kojic acid paired with tranexamic acid and niacinamide). Reported effect: Softening of post-inflammatory marks within a combination. Caveat: A combination result, not kojic acid alone.
- Tyrosinase inhibition (mechanism / in-vitro potency): grade B.
