Evidence base: Saeedi 2022 review — among the most consistent tyrosinase inhibitors in comparative in-vitro work, acting by chelating the enzyme's copper cofactor. Reported effect: Strong, consistent enzyme inhibition in the lab. Caveat: This grades the MECHANISM, not clinical proof; lab potency does not equal a cosmetic outcome.
- Standalone / EU-compliant-strength efficacy: grade C. Evidence base: EU Regulation 2024/996 caps kojic acid at 1%; most efficacy studies used 2–4%. Reported effect: Compliant products are milder than the studied strengths. Caveat: Isolated standalone evidence at a 1% compliant strength is limited.
- Tolerability & stability: grade not graded. Evidence base: Nakagawa 1995 — kojic acid is the brightener most associated with allergic contact dermatitis; it is also chemically unstable and photosensitising. Reported effect: Higher irritation and sensitisation risk than gentler brighteners. Caveat: A caution, not a benefit. Patch test; well-packaged (opaque, air-tight) products matter because it oxidises.
Legal status (EU): Restricted — maximum 1% in leave-on face and hand products. EU Regulation 2024/996 (the same package that capped retinol at 0.3%) limits kojic acid to a maximum of 1% in leave-on face and hand products, after an SCCS review that concluded 1% is safe for consumers. Older non-compliant products were phased out. Much of kojic acid's strongest efficacy evidence comes from 2–4%, so an EU-compliant product is deliberately milder than what the studies tested.
Legal status (INT): Lawful cosmetic ingredient. Kojic acid is a long-established, globally recognised cosmetic brightening ingredient. Concentration limits and rules vary by region; the EU cap of 1% is the notable restriction.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): for the appearance of a brighter, more even complexion; helps reduce the look of dark spots; brightening support within a combination routine.
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: treats melasma; treats hyperpigmentation; cures dark spots; bleaches skin; inhibits tyrosinase; skin-whitening.
Honest note: Kojic acid is a cosmetic ingredient, framed around the appearance of tone. In the EU, Regulation 2024/996 caps it at a maximum of 1% in leave-on face and hand products; most efficacy studies used 2–4%, so EU-compliant products are milder than what was tested. It is the brightener most associated with allergic contact dermatitis, and it is unstable and photosensitising — so patch testing, well-sealed opaque packaging, and daily sunscreen matter more here than with gentler brighteners. Persistent or hormonal pigmentation such as melasma is a dermatologist's territory, and pregnancy use is best discussed with a doctor.
Identity: a compound produced by fungi during fermentation — its skin-brightening power was first noticed on the pale hands of sake brewers. It is a long-established, globally recognised depigmenting agent, and also chemically unstable (prone to oxidising and losing potency) and, in the EU, now regulated: Regulation 2024/996 — the same package that capped retinol at 0.3% — limits kojic acid to a maximum of 1% in leave-on face and hand products. For the full readable explanation of what it is and how it works, see the companion guide, what is kojic acid?
Mechanism (as proposed): it inhibits tyrosinase by chelating the copper ion the enzyme needs to function — disarming the pigment machinery by removing a critical part rather than jamming the assembly line. In comparative laboratory work it is one of the most consistent tyrosinase inhibitors, reducing melanin without cytotoxicity in cell studies. The mechanistic case is genuinely strong; the gap, as always, is between a clean lab result and a real-world cosmetic outcome — widened here by the EU 1% cap (most studies used 2–4%), its instability, and its tendency to be used in combination rather than alone.
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 3 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/kojic-acid (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: KPV
title: KPV — the anti-inflammatory α-MSH fragment: evidence & status
text:
KPV — peptide.
Also known as: KPV, Lys-Pro-Val (Lysine-Proline-Valine), α-MSH(11-13), the C-terminal tripeptide of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone. MW ~342 Da. INCI: none.
KPV is the anti-inflammatory C-terminal tripeptide of α-MSH — the same hormone behind the melanotan and PT-141 peptides, but stripped of all melanocortin-receptor activity (no tanning, no appetite effect). It works intracellularly via the PepT1 transporter to block NF-κB, which makes it a gut/IBD-focused anti-inflammatory that can even be given orally. It has two decades of preclinical data (strongest in colitis models) but no human clinical trials, and it's currently under FDA compounding review (July 2026).
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Anti-inflammatory / colitis / IBD (preclinical): grade C. Evidence base: Substantial preclinical (Dalmasso PepT1; Kannengiesser 2008 DSS/TNBS; Xiao 2017 nanoparticle). Reported effect: Reduces intestinal inflammation. Caveat: Strong, mechanistically well-characterised animal data — but no human trials.
- PepT1 / NF-κB mechanism: grade not graded. Evidence base: Dalmasso et al. + PepT1-knockout confirmation. Reported effect: Genuine, receptor-independent. Caveat: Established preclinically; not melanocortin-mediated.
- Human use (IBD, dermatitis, gut healing — the marketed use): grade F. Evidence base: None. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Zero completed human RCTs in any indication.
- Antimicrobial (S. aureus, C. albicans): grade D. Evidence base: Preclinical. Reported effect: Suggestive. Caveat: Lab/preclinical.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Preclinical + preliminary. Reported effect: Favourable so far. Caveat: No melanocortin activity (so no pigmentation/appetite/BP effects); stable; but no human safety data.
Legal status (US): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). Not FDA-approved. In the July 2026 PCAC review batch for 503A compounding (regulatory status in flux through the 2025–2026 review). Sold gray-market as a "research" peptide (often for gut health, sometimes alongside BPC-157).
Legal status (EU): Not approved. not approved.
Reference entry — not sold here. The intrigue: it's a fragment of the same hormone behind the tanning and libido peptides (α-MSH) — but it's the anti-inflammatory tail that does NOT act like a melanocortin at all. In the July 2026 FDA PCAC batch alongside BPC-157, TB-500 and MOTS-c. No dosing published here.
Status note: FDA compounding status evolving — re-check the July PCAC outcome
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/kpv (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Larazotide (AT-1001, Larazotide Acetate)
title: Larazotide (AT-1001) — the tight-junction "leaky gut" peptide that reached Phase 3 and failed: the honest story
text:
Larazotide (AT-1001, Larazotide Acetate) — peptide.
Also known as: Larazotide, larazotide acetate, AT-1001, INN-202.
Larazotide (AT-1001) is a synthetic octapeptide and a first-in-class "tight-junction regulator" — the first drug ever aimed at intestinal permeability, the "leaky gut" concept. Taken orally and acting only inside the gut, it works as a zonulin antagonist: in celiac disease, gluten makes the junctions between intestinal cells spring open (via zonulin), letting gliadin through to trigger the immune attack — and larazotide is designed to hold those junctions shut. It's genuinely notable for how far it got: developed across three companies (Alba → Innovate → 9 Meters), it became the most clinically advanced non-dietary celiac drug, the only Phase 3-stage one, with FDA Fast Track and positive Phase 2b data. But in June 2022 its pivotal Phase 3 (CeDLara) was stopped for futility — an interim analysis showed it couldn't feasibly beat placebo — a major blow to the whole zonulin/"leaky gut" drug hypothesis. As of 2026 it's approved for nothing. The honest, and broadly important, takeaway: the most rigorously developed "leaky gut" drug in history failed its decisive trial — which should temper the far larger, far less tested wellness-market hype around "leaky gut" supplements. A real drug, a real mechanism, a real (negative) answer.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Celiac disease (the lead, best-studied use): grade D. Evidence base: Positive Phase 2b (342 pts) → but Phase 3 CeDLara discontinued for futility (2022). Caveat: The defining fact: reached Phase 3 and failed — couldn't feasibly beat placebo. Not "untested" — tested and negative at the pivotal stage.
- Tight-junction / permeability mechanism: grade C. Evidence base: Well-characterized: zonulin antagonism; MLCK inhibition; rodent + porcine barrier-repair models. Caveat: The biology of tight-junction regulation is real and elegant; it didn't translate to a positive clinical endpoint.
- "Leaky gut" / general gut health (the gray-market pitch): grade F. Evidence base: No approved indication; concept-level only. Caveat: The broad wellness claim is not supported; even the targeted celiac trial failed.
- Adjacent indications (MIS-C, intestinal ischemia): grade F. Evidence base: Early/preclinical (porcine ischemia repair, pediatric research). Caveat: Exploratory; nothing established.
- Safety: grade B. Evidence base: Extensive trial exposure; gut-restricted (minimal systemic absorption). Caveat: Generally well-tolerated in trials (a genuine plus — its problem was efficacy, not safety); long-term chronic-modulation effects unestablished.
Legal status (US): Not approved. not approved for any indication (the Phase 3 celiac program failed in 2022). It had FDA Fast Track during development, but never reached approval. Investigational.
The "leaky gut" drug that got furthest — and still failed Phase 3. Larazotide is unusual in this register: a first-in-class, FDA Fast-Tracked, Phase 3-stage pharmaceutical — the most clinically advanced non-dietary celiac-disease drug ever — not a typical gray-market peptide. Three threads: (1) its mechanism is genuinely novel — it's the first drug to target intestinal permeability ("leaky gut") by tightening the junctions between gut cells; (2) it reached Phase 3 with positive Phase 2 data and a real pipeline; (3) in June 2022 that Phase 3 was discontinued for futility — a major setback for the whole zonulin/"leaky gut" pharmacologic hypothesis. The most-developed leaky-gut drug in history couldn't beat placebo — a sobering fact for the supplement hype around the same concept.
Status note: status: celiac Phase 3 failed 2022, no approved indication; watch only for revived development in adjacent barrier-disease indications
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 2 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/larazotide (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Liraglutide
title: Liraglutide (Victoza / Saxenda) — evidence, the pioneer story & status
text:
Liraglutide — peptide.
Also known as: liraglutide, GLP-1 receptor agonist, brands Victoza (type-2 diabetes), Saxenda (obesity), Saxenda (obesity brand), Victoza (type-2 diabetes brand).
Liraglutide is the pioneer GLP-1 — the first in wide use and the first approved for obesity (Saxenda, 2014), the drug that opened the field for semaglutide and tirzepatide. It is FDA-approved for type-2 diabetes, obesity and cardiovascular risk (grade A), and in 2024–2025 became the first GLP-1 to go generic, meaningfully widening access. Its trade-offs are honest: it's a daily injection and less potent than the newer drugs (~8% weight loss vs semaglutide ~15% and tirzepatide ~22%) — eclipsed on efficacy, but proven, long-tracked, and now affordable.
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Type-2 diabetes glycemic control: grade A. Evidence base: Phase 3 (LEAD programme); FDA-approved. Reported effect: Robust HbA1c reduction. Caveat: Slightly less than semaglutide (SUSTAIN-10).
- Chronic weight management (obesity): grade A. Evidence base: SCALE Phase 3 (~8.4% at 56 wk); FDA-approved (first GLP-1 for obesity). Reported effect: Meaningful weight loss. Caveat: Less than semaglutide (~15%) / tirzepatide (~22%); daily injection; head-to-head STEP 8 = 6.4% vs 15.8%.
- Cardiovascular risk reduction (T2D + CVD): grade A. Evidence base: LEADER (n=9,340); FDA-approved. Reported effect: 13% reduction in major CV events. Caveat: In T2D + established CVD only — not generalisable to non-diabetic obesity (unlike semaglutide's SELECT).
- Pediatric obesity (adolescents 12+): grade A. Evidence base: Kelly 2020 (NEJM); FDA-approved. Reported effect: Reduced BMI standard-deviation score. Caveat: Longest pediatric track record in the class.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Large trials + 15+ years post-marketing. Reported effect: Well-characterised (longest record in class). Caveat: GI effects; gallbladder events (1–2%); rare pancreatitis; boxed thyroid C-cell warning (rodent); more injection-site reactions (7× the injections of a weekly drug).
Legal status (US): Approved / prescription. FDA- and EMA-approved (Victoza, Saxenda, and generics). Unlike semaglutide/tirzepatide, it is not caught in the shortage/compounding situation — approved generics exist.
Reference entry — NOT a sellable lane. An FDA-approved prescription drug (now also generic), not a cosmetic or research peptide. It earns its place in the register for two reasons that are genuinely historic: it was the first GLP-1 approved for obesity, and it was the first GLP-1 to go generic. No dosing published here.
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/liraglutide (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: LL-37 (Cathelicidin)
title: LL-37 (cathelicidin) — the double-edged antimicrobial peptide: healer, and driver of rosacea/psoriasis
text:
LL-37 (Cathelicidin) — peptide.
Also known as: LL-37, cathelicidin (the human cathelicidin), the C-terminal active fragment of hCAP18 (gene CAMP).
LL-37 is the body's own broad-spectrum antimicrobial peptide — the only human cathelicidin — a 37-amino-acid innate-immune effector made by skin and immune cells, switched on by vitamin D and injury, and activated by protease cleavage. It genuinely kills microbes (by disrupting their membranes) and supports wound healing and angiogenesis. But its defining feature is a dual role: it's also an "alarmin," and when its expression or processing goes wrong, it becomes a driver of inflammatory and autoimmune disease — it generates the inflammatory fragments of rosacea, breaks tolerance to self-DNA in psoriasis (via TLR9), and feeds lupus. That's the paradox for a register: LL-37 is sold as a healing/antimicrobial peptide, but its largest, most rigorous evidence base is about how it causes skin disease when dysregulated. As an exogenous therapeutic it's preclinical with no approvals, and administering a pro-inflammatory alarmin — particularly to anyone prone to rosacea or psoriasis — could plausibly do the opposite of healing. A fascinating, important molecule; a genuinely double-edged one; not a validated therapy.
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Endogenous antimicrobial defense: grade A. Evidence base: Extensive: potent broad-spectrum membrane-disrupting killing; surges in wounds. Caveat: This grades LL-37's natural role — NOT a demonstrated benefit of taking exogenous LL-37.
- Exogenous wound healing / antimicrobial therapy (the marketed use): grade D. Evidence base: Preclinical / early; angiogenic & pro-healing in models. Caveat: No approved product; no human trials establishing efficacy or safety of exogenous use.
- Role in rosacea (as a cause): grade A. Evidence base: Strong: abnormal processing → pro-inflammatory fragments → erythema/telangiectasia. Caveat: This is evidence it drives disease — a reason for caution, not a therapeutic credential.
- Role in psoriasis / lupus (as a cause): grade A. Evidence base: Strong: LL-37+self-DNA→TLR9→break self-tolerance; T-cell autoantigen; NET/IFN in SLE. Caveat: Again: robust evidence of harm/pathogenic role, underscoring the risk of exogenous use.
- Safety of exogenous administration: grade not graded. Evidence base: Not established in humans. Caveat: It's a pro-inflammatory alarmin; plausible to trigger/worsen inflammatory-autoimmune skin disease; contraindicated intuition for rosacea/psoriasis-prone users.
Legal status (EU): not an approved drug. not an approved drug; investigational. Not an approved cosmetic ingredient (an injectable pro-inflammatory alarmin is not a topical cosmetic). No compounding pathway.
The double-edged sword of innate immunity — a natural antibiotic that, dysregulated, drives inflammatory disease. LL-37 is unusual in this register: it's sold as a healing/antimicrobial peptide, yet its largest and strongest evidence base is about its role in causing skin disease (rosacea, psoriasis, lupus). Three threads: (1) it's the body's own broad-spectrum antimicrobial peptide and a genuine innate-immune effector; (2) it has a "dual role" — antimicrobial and an "alarmin" that, when mis-processed or over-expressed, triggers inflammation, redness, and autoimmunity; (3) that means exogenous LL-37, marketed as healing, can plausibly feed the very inflammatory conditions its biology is famous for — the opposite of the intended pitch.
Status note: watch: engineered LL-37 analogs/fragments that dissociate antimicrobial from pro-inflammatory activity — the field's key therapeutic goal
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 1 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/ll-37 (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Matrixyl (Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4)
title: Matrixyl (Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4) — evidence, uses & the "collagen boosting" claim
text:
Matrixyl (Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4) — peptide.
Also known as: Matrixyl® (trade name, Sederma / Croda), INCI: Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4, Pal-KTTKS, Palmitoyl Oligopeptide (older INCI, Matrixyl-family peptides), formerly "Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-3" (pre-2006), Palmitoyl-Lys-Thr-Thr-Lys-Ser.
Matrixyl (INCI Palmitoyl Pentapeptide-4) is a synthetic signal peptide — a palmitoylated fragment (Pal-KTTKS) of type I procollagen — used in anti-ageing cosmetics. It has the most-replicated clinical evidence among cosmetic signal peptides, anchored by a 12-week split-face RCT, showing a modest improvement in the appearance of photoaged skin. Many of the larger figures quoted for it (e.g. "+117% collagen") come from in-vitro or manufacturer data, not the controlled human trial.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Topical: appearance of photoaged skin (fine lines, wrinkle depth, roughness): grade B. Evidence base: Robinson 2005 — 12-week split-face, double-blind, vehicle-controlled RCT, n=93. Reported effect: Statistically significant improvement vs placebo. Caveat: Best-evidenced cosmetic peptide of this set, but effect is modest; single main RCT; small.
- ECM synthesis in vitro (collagen I/III, fibronectin, HA; MMP-1↓, TIMP-1↑): grade B. Evidence base: Fibroblast assays at ~3 ppm. Reported effect: Substantial stimulation. Caveat: In vitro; the eye-catching "+117%/+327%" figures are cell-culture, not human outcomes.
- Comparable to retinol: grade C. Evidence base: Some measures vs low-dose retinol (~0.07%). Reported effect: Comparable on select endpoints, far gentler. Caveat: Not equivalent to prescription retinoids; comparison is to low-concentration retinol.
- Skin penetration at cosmetic doses: grade not graded. Evidence base: Efficacy seen at 3–5 ppm; formal dose-escalation not published. Reported effect: Palmitoyl tail improves penetration. Caveat: Better than plain KTTKS, but a formal 1–50 ppm dose study is lacking.
- Safety (topical): grade not graded. Evidence base: Broad cosmetic use. Reported effect: Well tolerated; no photosensitivity. Caveat: Non-irritating; suitable for sensitive skin.
Legal status (EU): Lawful cosmetic. a lawful cosmetic ingredient (EU Regulation (EC) 1223/2009; US cosmetic law), sold over-the-counter worldwide. No injectable or "research" lane, no drug filing. Straightforwardly sellable as a cosmetic — the constraint is the claims.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): for the **appearance** of firmer, plumper, smoother skin; helps reduce the **look** of fine lines and wrinkles; supports a more youthful-looking complexion.
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: boosts / stimulates / increases **collagen** (synthesis/production); collagen-boosting; rebuilds collagen; by function; wound healing; repairs; regenerates; the proposed mechanism.
Cosmetic signal peptide — a different archetype from Argireline. Argireline is a neuromodulator (proposed to relax muscle); Matrixyl is a matrikine / signal peptide (proposed to tell skin to build matrix). It is the best-evidenced of the common cosmetic peptides — and its whole marketing ("collagen-boosting") is the claim trap.
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 2 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/matrixyl (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Mazdutide (IBI362 / LY3305677)
title: Mazdutide (IBI362) — the first approved GLP-1/glucagon dual agonist: ~20% weight loss, the evidence
text:
Mazdutide (IBI362 / LY3305677) — peptide.
Also known as: Mazdutide, development codes IBI362 and LY3305677, a GLP-1 / glucagon (GCGR) dual receptor agonist, an oxyntomodulin (OXM) analog. Marketed in China (Innovent). INCI: none.
Mazdutide is a GLP-1 / glucagon dual-agonist peptide — the same mechanism as Survodutide (#6) — but designed as an oxyntomodulin analog and co-developed by Innovent and Eli Lilly. Its defining fact: in June 2025 it became the first GLP-1/glucagon dual agonist approved anywhere in the world (China NMPA, for weight management; T2D followed in September). The glucagon arm gives it the class-typical extras — increased energy expenditure and hepatic fat oxidation, plus notable visceral-fat reduction — on top of GLP-1 appetite suppression. Efficacy is strong: GLORY-2 reached ~20.1% weight loss at over 60 weeks (the tirzepatide tier) with only a two-step dose titration, and in the DREAMS-3 head-to-head it beat semaglutide on both weight and HbA1c. Tolerability is the usual GLP-1 GI profile (nausea/diarrhea/vomiting, mostly mild-moderate and transient; ~2.9% discontinuation in GLORY-2). The honest caveat: the pivotal evidence is entirely in Chinese populations, and it is approved only in China — global trials/approvals are still pending.
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Weight loss (obesity, no diabetes): grade A. Evidence base: Phase 3 GLORY-2 (2025): ~20.1% at / 60 wk; GLORY-1 (NEJM 2025) positive → China NMPA approval. Caveat: Approved, strong efficacy — but pivotal data are Chinese-population only; not approved/validated elsewhere yet. The "A" is region-qualified.
- Glycemic control (T2D): grade A. Evidence base: DREAMS-1/-2 (Nature, 2025) vs placebo; DREAMS-3 head-to-head superior to semaglutide → NMPA T2D approval. Caveat: Approved for T2D in China; head-to-head win is notable; same population caveat.
- Visceral / hepatic fat: grade B. Evidence base: Glucagon-arm effect; reported waist-circumference, lipid, BP improvements. Caveat: Consistent metabolic benefits reported; dedicated MASH-histology program less developed than Survodutide's.
- Cardiometabolic risk factors (BP, lipids, waist): grade B. Evidence base: Phase 3 secondary endpoints. Caveat: Improvements reported alongside weight/glucose; long-term CV-outcome trial not the headline here.
- Safety / tolerability: grade not graded. Evidence base: Phase 1b→3 (GLORY/DREAMS). Caveat: GI predominant (nausea ~21–41%, diarrhea, vomiting — dose-escalation, then settle); mild-moderate; only 2.9% discontinued in GLORY-2; glucagon arm still requires glucose-balanced titration (like all GCGR/GLP-1 duals).
Legal status (EU): Not approved. not approved outside China; investigational elsewhere. Not a cosmetic; no compounding pathway.
Survodutide's twin — same mechanism, but this one is already approved. Three points make this entry land: (1) it's the same GLP-1 / glucagon dual class as Survodutide (#6), but built as an oxyntomodulin analog — and, crucially, it is the first GLP-1/glucagon dual agonist approved anywhere in the world (China NMPA, 2025); (2) its Phase 3 GLORY-2 hit ~20.1% weight loss — matching the tirzepatide tier — with just a two-step titration; (3) in a head-to-head trial it beat semaglutide (DREAMS-3). This is the entry that shows "investigational vs approved" within the same mechanism.
Status note: China-approved; watch for global (non-Chinese) trials and any ex-China regulatory filing, which would change the region-qualified grades
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/mazdutide (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Melanotan II
title: Melanotan II — the tanning peptide: evidence, the discovery story & serious risks
text:
Melanotan II — peptide.
Also known as: Melanotan II, MT-II, MT-2, "the tanning peptide", a synthetic α-MSH analog. INCI: none.
Melanotan II is a non-selective melanocortin agonist — the original "tanning peptide." It genuinely does induce a tan (and arousal), but it is not approved anywhere, sits in FDA Category 2 for safety risks, and is linked in case reports to serious harms — priapism, mole/pigment changes, rhabdomyolysis, kidney injury and more. "Safe sunless tanning" is a dangerous misframing. Its erectile side effect gave rise to the FDA-approved drug PT-141 (#46), and its relative Melanotan I (afamelanotide) is separately approved for a rare disease — but MT-II itself is the unapproved, higher-risk one.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Skin tanning (the marketed use): grade C. Evidence base: Small human studies (Dorr 1996; Phase 1 tanning). Reported effect: Genuine, dose-dependent tanning. Caveat: The effect is real, but no completed safety/efficacy trial; not approved; efficacy is not the problem — safety is.
- Erectile / arousal effect: grade C. Evidence base: Small trials (Wessells 1998). Reported effect: Erection induction. Caveat: This effect became PT-141's domain; MT-II itself unapproved.
- "Safe sunless tanning" claim: grade F. Evidence base: —. Reported effect: Misframing. Caveat: The risk profile below makes "safe" inaccurate.
- Safety / serious adverse events: grade not graded. Evidence base: Case-report literature + trial AEs. Reported effect: Serious concerns. Caveat: Documented in case reports: priapism (sometimes needing surgery), changing/darkening moles (complicating melanoma detection), rhabdomyolysis, renal infarction, posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES); plus routine nausea/flushing. Whether MT-II causes melanoma is unproven and confounded by users' extra UV-seeking — but the pigment/mole changes are a real monitoring problem.
Legal status (US): Not FDA-approved (503A Cat. 2). FDA classifies MT-II in Category 2 of the 503A bulks list (a substance that raises significant safety risks for compounding).
Legal status (EU): illegal to sell for human use. illegal to sell for human use; nonetheless widely available through internet vendors and gym/salon networks, marketed as a tan.
Reference entry — not sold here. Melanotan II is not approved anywhere, sits in FDA Category 2 (significant safety risks), and is illegal to sell for human use in most countries. Unusually for this register, the marketed effect (tanning) is real — but the compound is genuinely unsafe, which is the whole point of the page. No dosing published here.
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 1 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/melanotan-ii (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: MGF / PEG-MGF (Mechano Growth Factor, IGF-1Ec)
title: MGF / PEG-MGF — the mechano-growth-factor muscle peptide: real splice biology, thin human evidence
text:
MGF / PEG-MGF (Mechano Growth Factor, IGF-1Ec) — peptide.
Also known as: MGF, Mechano Growth Factor, IGF-1Ec (human; IGF-1Eb in rodents), the IGF-1 E-peptide (Ec), PEG-MGF / PEGylated MGF (the long-acting form). INCI: none.
MGF (Mechano Growth Factor, IGF-1Ec) is a splice variant of IGF-1 produced locally in muscle in response to mechanical stress or damage. Its distinctive part is a 24-amino-acid E-peptide that acts as an early "first responder" repair signal — it wakes up quiescent satellite cells (muscle stem cells) and makes them proliferate, a job the ordinary circulating IGF-1 doesn't do well. In the natural repair cascade, this MGF pulse comes first (within hours), expanding the stem-cell pool, and is followed by a switch to IGF-1Ea, which drives the actual fiber growth. The biology is elegant and real — but it's overwhelmingly preclinical. Native MGF has an extremely short half-life (~5-7 minutes) by design (it's meant to be a local, transient signal), which is why the gray market sells PEG-MGF (PEGylated, longer-acting) — a form that carries batch-to-batch inconsistency and still lacks meaningful human trials. Add no long-term safety data on artificially activating muscle stem cells, and the honest read is: interesting mechanism, minimal human proof.
Overall evidence grade: D (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Satellite-cell (muscle stem-cell) activation / proliferation: grade D. Evidence base: Well-characterised in cell/animal models — MGF E-peptide activates quiescent satellite cells where mature IGF-1 barely does. Caveat: Genuinely distinct, reproducible preclinical biology — but not demonstrated as a human therapy.
- Muscle repair / hypertrophy (the marketed use): grade F. Evidence base: Animal / model data on the MGF→IGF-1Ea repair cascade. Caveat: Very limited human data; no controlled human hypertrophy/repair trials establishing efficacy.
- Aged-muscle / regeneration: grade F. Evidence base: Observation: aged muscle shows a blunted MGF response to loading vs young. Caveat: Interesting rationale (maybe why older muscle repairs slower) — but human interventional data absent.
- PEG-MGF as a practical agent: grade not graded. Evidence base: PK: PEGylation extends half-life from ~minutes to ~hours/≈24 h. Caveat: Solves the half-life problem mechanically, but subcutaneous kinetics not formally characterised, and unregulated PEG products vary widely batch-to-batch.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Little formal human data. Caveat: Injection-site pain/swelling common; no long-term data on chronically/artificially activating muscle stem cells; theoretical concerns (scar tissue, unregulated growth signalling) unquantified.
Legal status (EU): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). not approved anywhere; investigational research peptide. Not a cosmetic; no compounding pathway.
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport as an IGF-1/growth-factor agent, it falls in WADA-prohibited growth-factor territory.
The "first responder" of muscle repair — elegant splice-variant biology, almost entirely preclinical. Three points define this entry: (1) it's a genuinely distinct piece of biology — an IGF-1 splice variant whose unique E-peptide activates muscle stem (satellite) cells, a different job from ordinary IGF-1; (2) native MGF has a ~5-7 minute half-life by design, so the gray market sells PEG-MGF (PEGylated) to extend it — introducing its own quality-control problem; (3) despite rich mechanism, human data are very limited and the "artificially switching on muscle stem cells" question has no long-term safety data.
Status note: watch for any controlled human data — the field is preclinical-heavy; that would move the grades
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/mgf (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: MK-677 (Ibutamoren)
title: MK-677 (Ibutamoren) — evidence, the oral-GH story & status
text:
MK-677 (Ibutamoren) — small_molecule.
Also known as: MK-677, MK-0677, Ibutamoren (mesylate), Merck code L-163,191, pediatric development name LUM-201. INCI: none. Not a peptide and not a SARM (see below).
MK-677 (ibutamoren) is an oral, non-peptide ghrelin-receptor agonist — the pill version of the injectable GHRPs. Across ~30 years of trials it reliably raises GH and IGF-1 and deepens slow-wave sleep, and it modestly increases lean mass — but without improving strength or physical function, and at a real metabolic cost (insulin resistance / elevated blood glucose). It's not FDA-approved (the FDA is recalling consumer products that contain it), it carries a congestive-heart-failure safety signal from an elderly trial, and it's banned in sport. It is not a peptide and not a SARM.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- GH / IGF-1 elevation: grade B. Evidence base: ~30 years of trials (Chapman 1996; Svensson 1998; many). Reported effect: Sustained rise in GH & IGF-1. Caveat: Well-established biomarker effect — which is not the same as a clinical benefit.
- Slow-wave (deep) sleep: grade B. Evidence base: Documented across trials. Reported effect: Meaningful increase in stage-IV sleep. Caveat: A genuine, often most-noticed effect.
- Fat-free (lean) mass: grade C. Evidence base: Nass 2008 (Ann Intern Med, 2 yr, n=65): FFM +1.1 kg. Reported effect: Modest lean-mass gain. Caveat: No strength or functional improvement — the key honest limit.
- Anti-aging / physique / strength (the popular use): grade F. Evidence base: Lean-mass gain doesn't translate to function; appetite drives fat gain. Reported effect: Not demonstrated for these goals. Caveat: The FFM gain ≠ performance; ghrelin-driven hunger causes fat gain in physique use.
- Failed / halted indications: grade not graded. Evidence base: Alzheimer's (Sevigny 2008 — no effect); hip-fracture trial (Adunsky 2011) terminated early on a CHF signal. Reported effect: Negative / safety-halted. Caveat: The congestive-heart-failure signal in elderly is a serious flag.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Multiple trials. Reported effect: Real concerns. Caveat: Insulin resistance / elevated fasting glucose (primary risk); lower-extremity edema; appetite → fat gain; modest prolactin rise; theoretical oncogenic concern (sustained IGF-1); CHF signal; WADA-banned.
Legal status (US): Not approved. Not FDA-approved for any indication (30 years on, still unapproved; Merck shelved it, and Lumos's LUM-201 is in pediatric development, not approved). The FDA is actively enforcing against unapproved consumer products — a consumer alert on "iKids-Growth" (Sept 2025) and a recall of "Agebox" products containing undeclared ibutamoren (March 2026). It is not a scheduled substance, but selling it in supplements is unlawful.
Legal status (EU): Not approved. not approved.
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport: WADA-prohibited at all times — Section S2 (GH secretagogues / ghrelin mimetics); directly detectable.
Reference entry — not sold here. The oral, non-peptide sibling of the injectable GHRPs: same ghrelin-receptor mechanism, but a pill. Included because the demand is enormous and it sits right next to the GH-peptide cluster — honestly tagged as not a peptide. No dosing published here.
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/mk-677 (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: MOTS-c
title: MOTS-c — evidence, uses & status
text:
MOTS-c — peptide.
Also known as: MOTS-c, "Mitochondrial Open Reading Frame of the 12S rRNA type-c", a mitochondrial-derived peptide (MDP). INCI: none.
MOTS-c is a 16-amino-acid mitochondrial-derived peptide, encoded in mtDNA and discovered in 2015, that regulates metabolism largely through the AMPK pathway. Its preclinical and mechanistic research is strong and coherent — multiple laboratories, top-tier journals — showing improved insulin sensitivity and metabolic homeostasis in animal models. But there are no published human efficacy RCTs of administering MOTS-c; in humans it remains interesting biology, not established therapy.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Insulin sensitivity / glucose metabolism / metabolic homeostasis: grade C. Evidence base: Strong preclinical — Lee 2015 (Cell Metab) + multiple rodent studies (diet-induced obesity, T1D/T2D models). Reported effect: Consistent improvement in animals. Caveat: Mechanistically coherent and multi-lab, but no human efficacy RCT.
- Exercise-mimetic / physical capacity: grade D. Evidence base: Rodent studies + human observation (Reynolds 2021, Nat Commun: exercise raises muscle MOTS-c ~12-fold). Reported effect: Suggestive. Caveat: "Mimics exercise" ≠ "replaces exercise"; no human administration trial.
- Aging / healthspan: grade D. Evidence base: Preclinical + observational (levels decline with age; restored in aged mice by injection). Reported effect: Suggestive. Caveat: Human data is correlational, not interventional.
- Marketed "anti-aging / metabolic fix" in humans: grade F. Evidence base: None. Reported effect: Not demonstrated. Caveat: Human efficacy unproven; hype outruns evidence.
- Safety & long-term: grade not graded. Evidence base: No human efficacy/safety RCT. Reported effect: Unknown. Caveat: WADA-banned; vendor guidance flags caution for cardiac arrhythmia, malignancy, pregnancy — i.e. unknown risk.
Legal status (EU): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). Not approved for human use; not eligible for magistral/officinal compounding. Research-chemical sale does not authorise human use.
Legal status (US): Not FDA-approved (under review). On the PCAC agenda for 23 July 2026 (with BPC-157/TB-500); removed from Category 2 in April 2026; FDA's pre-meeting briefing documents proposed not adding it to the 503A Bulks List. Advisory only; no final rule. Not approved.
Legal status (INT): Approved nowhere. Approved nowhere.
WADA: prohibited in sport. Sport: treated as WADA-prohibited (metabolic modulator / AMPK-activating "exercise-mimetic", Section S4). Athletes should treat it as banned.
Research / reference — not for sale. No dosing, reconstitution, or administration is published (intentional). Neutral scientific reference only. Strong basic-science research; note the gap between mechanism/preclinical and human clinical proof.
Status note: US review status evolving — re-check the July PCAC outcome and any Federal Register rule
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 1 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/mots-c (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: NAD+ (and its precursors NMN / NR)
title: NAD+ / NMN / NR — the longevity coenzyme: elegant theory, real biomarker rise, unproven human benefit
text:
NAD+ (and its precursors NMN / NR) — small_molecule.
Also known as: NAD⁺ (nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide).
NAD⁺ is a fundamental coenzyme (not a peptide) that powers energy metabolism, DNA repair, and the sirtuin "longevity" enzymes — and declines with age, which is the basis of a very elegant theory: top NAD⁺ back up (via precursors like NMN or NR) and you might slow aging. That theory, and Harvard scientist David Sinclair's public NMN use, helped build a multi-hundred-million-dollar supplement industry. The science is honest but sobering: NMN/NR reliably and safely raise blood NAD⁺, and the animal data are genuinely impressive — but in humans, a 2025 meta-analysis of 10 RCTs found no benefit for muscle, strength, or physical function, and metabolic trials mostly haven't reproduced the mouse results (promising signals exist in specific patient groups — prediabetes, Parkinson's, PAD — not healthy people). Add a female-only mouse-lifespan effect and a shaky resveratrol pairing, and the picture is: a real, important molecule with a reliable biomarker effect and a real evidence gap on the outcomes that matter. Whether that gap is "doesn't work" or "trials too short" (Sinclair's view) is genuinely unresolved. It anchors this register's NAD⁺ biology — the pathway that MOTS-c (#10), 5-Amino-1MQ (#59), and SLU-PP-332 (#60) all feed into.
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Raising NAD⁺ levels (the biomarker): grade A. Evidence base: Multiple RCTs: NMN/NR reliably ↑ blood NAD⁺ (~40–59% at modest doses), safely. Caveat: This grades "does it raise NAD⁺" — YES. It does not mean the downstream health claims are proven.
- Physical function / muscle / strength (healthy older adults): grade F. Evidence base: 2025 meta-analysis, 10 RCTs: no benefit. Caveat: The clearest negative: marker up, function unchanged.
- Metabolic health (general): grade D. Evidence base: Human trials mostly failed to reproduce animal results. Caveat: Some signals; overall "iffy" per researchers.
- Specific clinical populations (prediabetes ♀, early Parkinson's, PAD): grade C. Evidence base: Small trials with some promising results. Caveat: Population-specific, preliminary, needs replication in large trials.
- Longevity / lifespan (humans): grade F. Evidence base: No human lifespan data; mouse data (female-biased). Caveat: Untestable short-term; the core claim is unproven in people.
- Safety: grade B. Evidence base: Extensive human exposure. Caveat: Generally well-tolerated and safe short-term (a genuine plus); long-term/high-dose and IV-specific safety less characterised; NMN regulatory status contested.
Legal status (EU): sold as a dietary supplement (NMN, NR, niacin/nicotinamide) . sold as a dietary supplement (NMN, NR, niacin/nicotinamide) and as IV NAD⁺ in wellness clinics. Note: NMN's US supplement status has been contested by the FDA (a regulatory grey area after NMN was investigated as a drug); NR is broadly sold. Not an approved anti-aging therapy anywhere.
The longevity supplement everyone's heard of — where the biomarker reliably moves but the human benefits don't (yet). This is the register's most mainstream, most commercially enormous entry, and a textbook "elegant theory, iffy human data" case. Three threads: (1) it's not a peptide — a fundamental coenzyme — and anchors the biology that several register peptides (#10, #59, #60) converge on; (2) its theory is genuinely elegant (NAD+ falls with age → sirtuins/DNA-repair suffer → restore it → slow aging), championed by Harvard's David Sinclair; (3) the honest catch — supplements (NMN/NR) reliably raise blood NAD+, but a 2025 meta-analysis of 10 RCTs found no benefit for muscle, strength, or physical function in older adults. The gap between "moves the marker" and "changes outcomes" is the whole story.
Status note: watch: longer/larger RCTs and the disease-specific trials — neurodegeneration, metabolic — that could finally close or confirm the biomarker-vs-outcome gap
Evidence provenance: 5 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/nad-plus (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Naringenin
title: Naringenin — evidence, uses & status
text:
Naringenin — small_molecule.
Also known as: 4′,5,7-trihydroxyflavanone, flavanone, grapefruit polyphenol, biotech-fermented naringenin (Debut / Deinde), INCI: Naringenin.
Naringenin is a flavanone polyphenol naturally present in citrus fruits (concentrated in grapefruit peel). Independent laboratory research (2016–2024) consistently shows it has meaningful biological activity on skin cells: anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidative, MMP-suppressing, UV- protective — and, uniquely among four common polyphenols in a 2024 comparison, superior anti-lipoperoxidation in stratum corneum. However, every one of these independent studies is either in-vitro (cells, reconstructed skin models) or on animals. The only human clinical trial is an in-house study by Debut Biotech / Deinde (n=30, 8 weeks, 0.45% naringenin), which is a promising signal but not independent proof of visible anti-aging effect. The claim "15× more effective than niacinamide" is a cell-culture single-biomarker comparison, not a head-to-head clinical trial. Naringenin is a plausible barrier-protective, gentle anti-oxidative ingredient — appropriate for sensitive and perimenopausal skin — but the strong marketing framing outruns the current independent evidence base.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Topical: anti-oxidative barrier protection: grade B. Evidence base: Ruscinc et al. 2024 (Frontiers in Chemistry) — comparative study of 4 polyphenols in 0.1% gels; naringenin the only one showing significant anti-lipoperoxidation in stratum corneum (others were pro-oxidant under UV stress). Reported effect: Reduction of UV-induced lipid peroxidation in stratum corneum; superior to chlorogenic acid, apigenin, kaempferol. Caveat: In vitro / ex vivo skin model; not a visible-outcome human RCT; independent replication with clinical endpoints still lacking.
- Topical: MMP-1 suppression / anti-photoaging (mechanism): grade B. Evidence base: Yin 2016 (J Cell Mol Med) — naringenin binds ERK2 and suppresses UVB-induced MMP-1 in cells and mice; Martinez 2016 (PLOS ONE) — topical naringenin cream reduced UVB-induced skin inflammation and oxidative damage in hairless mice; Martinez 2023 (Cosmetics MDPI) — UVB and pollution protection in human skin cells. Reported effect: Consistent anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidative, MMP-suppressing activity across independent labs. Caveat: All independent evidence is in-vitro (cells, reconstructed epidermis) or animal (mice); no independent human RCT with visible endpoints.
- Anti-inflammatory (skin senescence / inflammaging mechanism): grade B. Evidence base: Kim 2018 (Biomedical Dermatology) — human dermal fibroblasts; naringenin activates SIRT1, blocks NF-κB, reduces MMP expression. Published 6 years before Deinde launch, no brand affiliation.. Reported effect: Reduction of inflammatory cytokines and MMPs in fibroblast cell models. Caveat: In vitro; effect in living skin harder to demonstrate; brand-affiliated claims of "15× more effective than niacinamide" derive from cell-culture single-biomarker comparison at unmatched concentrations — not head-to-head clinical.
- Visible anti-aging / inflammaging (finished product): grade C. Evidence base: Deinde in-house RCT — 8 weeks, placebo-controlled, double-blind, n=30, 0.45% naringenin serum; 100% moisture/elasticity, 97% fine-line reduction reported. Reported effect: Improvement in subject-assessed and instrumental endpoints. Caveat: Small sample size; in-house / brand-run; no independent replication; "% of participants improved" does not report magnitude of change.
- Pigmentation / melanogenesis (in-vitro): grade C. Evidence base: Martinez 2023 — pigmented reconstructed epidermis; naringenin reduced pigmentation-related gene expression and melanin content. Reported effect: In-vitro pigmentation reduction. Caveat: Not translated to clinical endpoints; other polyphenols show similar in-vitro effects that do not translate to visible in vivo results.
- Safety (topical): grade not graded. Evidence base: Ruscinc 2024 HET-CAM assay — non-irritating; decades of dietary exposure (citrus consumption); no significant topical safety signals. Reported effect: Well tolerated. Caveat: Photostability of the molecule under UV requires appropriate formulation; grapefruit-derived versus biotech-fermented forms are chemically identical.
Legal status (INT): Lawful cosmetic ingredient. Topical Naringenin is a lawful cosmetic ingredient in the EU (Regulation (EC) 1223/2009), UK, US, and Asia. CosIng-listed. Biotech- fermented naringenin (produced by Debut Biotech and used exclusively in the Deinde brand) is chemically identical to plant-extracted naringenin and follows the same regulatory pathway. Not restricted.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): for the appearance of calm, healthy-looking skin; supports the look of a resilient skin barrier; antioxidant; helps skin look protected from environmental stress.
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: reverses aging; interrupts the root cause of skin aging; 15 times more effective than niacinamide; reduces inflammation; anti-inflammatory; stops inflammaging; inhibits MMP; protects collagen (as a stand-alone claim).
Naringenin is a lawful cosmetic ingredient globally. Vallydia does not currently sell a naringenin product — the biotech-fermented form is exclusively formulated into the Deinde brand by Debut Biotech, and plant- extracted forms of varying purity are used by other brands. This registry entry provides independent evidence-grading as a reference; the reference science below spans both cell/animal mechanism studies and one in-house brand clinical trial.
Identity: a flavanone — specifically 4′,5,7-trihydroxyflavanone — a subclass of flavonoid polyphenols. Naturally present in citrus fruits, most concentrated in the peel and pith of grapefruit and other citrus. Bitter taste characteristic (naringin, the glycoside form, is responsible for grapefruit bitterness; naringenin is the aglycone). Also produced by biotech fermentation using engineered microorganisms — Debut Biotech's process yields ~99% pure naringenin used exclusively in their Deinde brand at 0.45% concentration.
Mechanism (as proposed): a flavonoid that acts through multiple documented pathways in skin cells. It binds directly to ERK2, blocking UVB-induced MMP-1 expression (mechanism suppressing collagen degradation, per Yin 2016). It activates SIRT1 and blocks NF-κB inflammatory signalling in dermal fibroblasts (mechanism reducing senescence-associated cytokines, per Kim 2018). It shows anti-oxidative activity — uniquely among common cosmetic polyphenols, it protects rather than damages stratum corneum lipids under UV stress (Ruscinc 2024). In pigmented reconstructed epidermis it reduces melanogenesis gene expression (Martinez 2023). The biotech marketing framing translates these mechanisms as "interrupting inflammaging" and "15× more effective than niacinamide" — these translations require caution: the cell-culture and animal evidence is real, but the extrapolation to visible anti-aging results on human faces currently rests on one in-house 30-person clinical study by the brand selling the product.
Evidence provenance: 6 cited sources, 3 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/naringenin (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3)
title: Niacinamide (Vitamin B3) — evidence, uses & status
text:
Niacinamide (Vitamin B3) — small_molecule.
Also known as: nicotinamide, vitamin B3, NAM, 3-pyridinecarboxamide, INCI: Niacinamide.
Niacinamide (nicotinamide, vitamin B3) is one of the best-evidenced cosmetic active ingredients. Multiple independent controlled human trials show measurable improvement in skin appearance related to hyperpigmentation, barrier function (reduced TEWL), and — at higher concentrations — fine lines and sebum. Effect sizes are modest compared with prescription retinoids or hydroquinone, but tolerability is much better and it plays well with almost every other active. It is a cornerstone cosmetic ingredient, not a hero molecule.
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Topical: hyperpigmentation (melasma, PIH, age spots) at 2–5%: grade A. Evidence base: Multiple double-blind, split-face, vehicle-controlled RCTs (Hakozaki 2002 n=18 and n=79 dose-response; Kimball 2010; 2005 sallowness split-face n=~200); recent 2025 narrative reviews confirm consistent effect. Reported effect: Measurable reduction of melanin transfer to keratinocytes; visible skin-tone improvement over 8–12 weeks. Caveat: Smaller than hydroquinone in head-to-head trials; better tolerated; typically requires 8+ weeks of twice-daily use.
- Barrier function / TEWL (2–5%): grade A. Evidence base: Controlled trials showing measurable ceramide/free fatty acid synthesis increase and TEWL reduction; Kimball 10-week trial with 4% NAM + N-acetylglucosamine. Reported effect: Improved stratum corneum integrity, reduced water loss. Caveat: Formulation and vehicle matter; mechanism robust in vitro and in vivo.
- Fine lines and appearance of photoaging (5–6%): grade B. Evidence base: RCT of 6% niacinamide formulation reporting ~15% fine-line reduction; JAAD split-face sallowness study. Reported effect: Modest visible improvement in appearance of fine lines and texture. Caveat: Smaller than tretinoin/retinoids; multi-active formulations make isolated effect hard to prove.
- Sebum/oiliness reduction (2–4%): grade B. Evidence base: Multiple controlled trials at 2% and 4% for 2–4 weeks. Reported effect: Measurable reduction in sebum excretion rate. Caveat: Effect modest; some trials mixed; regional variability (better in Asian studies).
- Acne vulgaris (topical 4%): grade B. Evidence base: Randomized trials vs clindamycin 1%; comparable efficacy on inflammatory lesions. Reported effect: Reduction in inflammatory lesion count. Caveat: Consumer skincare framing: use as appearance/comfort adjunct, not medicine.
- Systemic / oral high-dose "anti-aging" claims: grade D. Evidence base: Small metabolic studies; oral NAM supplementation for photoprotection. Reported effect: Oral 500 mg BID reduced non-melanoma skin cancer incidence in ONTRAC trial; unrelated to cosmetic topical claims. Caveat: Systemic dosing outside cosmetic scope; do not conflate oral clinical data with topical serum claims.
- Safety (topical): grade not graded. Evidence base: Decades of widespread cosmetic use; controlled trials. Reported effect: Generally well tolerated. Caveat: Occasional mild flushing/tingling at higher concentrations; rare contact reactions; concentrations >10% not shown to add benefit.
Legal status (INT): Lawful cosmetic ingredient. Topical Niacinamide is a lawful cosmetic ingredient globally, including EU (Regulation (EC) 1223/2009), UK, US, and Asia. CosIng-listed. No concentration ceiling in the EU cosmetic framework; consumer formulas typically use 2–10%. Not a controlled or restricted substance.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): for the appearance of a more even, brighter-looking complexion; helps improve the look of uneven skin tone; supports the appearance of a healthy skin barrier; for the look of smoother, more refined-looking skin; antioxidant.
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: treats melasma; treats hyperpigmentation; treats acne; inhibits melanogenesis; repairs the skin barrier; stimulates ceramide synthesis; reduces inflammation; anti-inflammatory; prevents skin cancer; reverses photoaging.
Niacinamide is a lawful cosmetic ingredient worldwide. Vallydia uses it in cosmetic serums at concentrations supported by clinical trials (2–10%) with appearance-only claims. Reference science below includes both topical cosmetic and higher-dose oral / medical uses; only topical cosmetic use is relevant to the sellable product.
Identity: a water-soluble amide form of vitamin B3 (pyridine-3-carboxamide). It is the biologically active form used by the body to synthesise NAD+ and NADP+ — coenzymes central to cellular energy metabolism, DNA repair, and hundreds of enzymatic reactions. In skincare it is applied topically as a small, stable, well-tolerated molecule.
Mechanism (as proposed): a small hydrophilic molecule that penetrates the stratum corneum readily. In keratinocytes it acts as a precursor to NAD+/NADP+, supporting cellular energy and repair. Documented mechanisms relevant to skin appearance include: reduced melanosome transfer from melanocytes to keratinocytes (mechanism behind the pigmentation effect); increased synthesis of ceramides and free fatty acids in the stratum corneum (mechanism behind the barrier effect); modulation of sebaceous lipogenesis; and antioxidant activity through NAD+-dependent enzymes.
Evidence provenance: 6 cited sources, 4 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/niacinamide (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Noopept (Omberacetam)
title: Noopept — evidence, why it's not a peptide & status
text:
Noopept (Omberacetam) — small_molecule.
Also known as: Noopept, GVS-111, omberacetam, N-phenylacetyl-L-prolylglycine ethyl ester. INCI: none.
Noopept is a dipeptide-derived small molecule (not a classical peptide, despite the ubiquitous label) developed in Russia and approved there as an oral nootropic. It's a prodrug of the endogenous dipeptide cycloprolylglycine, raises BDNF/NGF, and is ~1000× more potent by weight than piracetam. Its strongest evidence is in people with existing cognitive deficits (vascular/traumatic) via Russian trials; the popular healthy-enhancement use and any Western evidence are thin, and it is not FDA-approved. Its practical edge over Semax/Selank is simply that it's oral.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Cognitive impairment / MCI (vascular, traumatic — approved Russian use): grade C. Evidence base: Russian RCTs (Neznamov & Teleshova 2009, vs piracetam) + EEG studies. Reported effect: Improved memory / cognition. Caveat: Strongest use, but Russian-only, no Western replication; methodology varies from FDA/EMA standards.
- BDNF/NGF & neuroprotection mechanism: grade B. Evidence base: Substantial preclinical (Ostrovskaya, Gudasheva). Reported effect: Neurotrophic; multi-target. Caveat: Animal/mechanistic; human translation is Russian-only.
- Cognitive enhancement in healthy adults (the popular use): grade D. Evidence base: Extrapolated + anecdotal. Reported effect: Suggestive. Caveat: Weaker than the cognitive-deficit use — the inverse of how it's marketed.
- Anti-amyloid / Alzheimer's: grade D. Evidence base: Cellular/animal models (Jia 2011; Ostrovskaya 2014). Reported effect: Suggestive. Caveat: Preclinical.
- Anxiolytic: grade D. Evidence base: Preclinical (cPG) + some Russian data. Reported effect: Suggestive. Caveat: Early.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Approved drug (Russia), oral, broad use. Reported effect: Generally well tolerated. Caveat: Mild (irritability, sleep changes, headache in some); long-term / Western data limited.
Legal status (US): Not FDA-approved (gray-market). Not FDA-approved. Sold as an unregulated "supplement" or research chemical (a legal gray area — the FDA does not recognise it as a lawful dietary ingredient, yet it's widely sold).
Legal status (EU): Not approved. not approved.
Legal status (INT): approval limited to Russia/CIS. approval limited to Russia/CIS.
Reference entry — not sold here. Like Semax (#41) and Selank (#42), an approved nootropic in Russia — and the oral one. No dosing published here.
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 1 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/noopept (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Orforglipron
title: Orforglipron — the first oral small-molecule GLP-1: evidence & status
text:
Orforglipron — small_molecule.
Also known as: orforglipron (or-for-GLIP-ron), Lilly code LY3502970, Chugai code OWL833.
Orforglipron is the first oral small-molecule GLP-1 — a non-peptide pill, taken anytime without food/water restrictions, that in Phase 3 delivered semaglutide-level weight loss (~11–12%) and solid glycemic control. Its significance is less about the size of the effect than about manufacturing and access: a cheaply mass-produced oral GLP-1 that Lilly says it can supply worldwide without constraint. Lilly filed the obesity NDA in December 2025; it is not yet approved.
Overall evidence grade: B (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Weight loss (obesity): grade B. Evidence base: Phase 3 ATTAIN-1 (NEJM 2025, ~11–12%); NDA filed Dec 2025. Reported effect: Meaningful; semaglutide-like. Caveat: Strong Phase 3 and FDA-filed, but not yet approved; efficacy is semaglutide-level, not tirzepatide-level.
- Type-2 diabetes glycemic control: grade B. Evidence base: Phase 3 ACHIEVE programme (5 trials, superior A1C; NEJM 2025). Reported effect: Robust, foundational. Caveat: Phase 3 complete/near-complete; T2D filing 2026; not yet approved.
- Weight maintenance after injectables: grade B. Evidence base: ATTAIN-MAINTAIN (Dec 2025). Reported effect: Maintained (switch from Wegovy/Zepbound). Caveat: First-of-its-kind; topline.
- Manufacturing / access advantage: grade not graded. Evidence base: Small-molecule chemistry. Reported effect: Cheap, scalable, no cold chain, no food/water ritual. Caveat: The reason it may matter more than its weight-loss number.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Phase 3 (GI-predominant). Reported effect: Consistent with the GLP-1 class. Caveat: Class-typical GI effects; investigational.
Legal status (INT): See note. - Everywhere — investigational, not approved. Obesity NDA submitted to the FDA (Dec 2025); T2D filing expected 2026. A potential 2026 approval would make it the first oral small-molecule GLP-1.
- Gray market: because it is a small molecule (easier to synthesise than a peptide), unregulated "research orforglipron" copies may proliferate faster than peptide knock-offs — and are just as unapproved and unverified.
- Sport: GLP-1 agonists are not currently on the WADA Prohibited List.
- This venture: not a sellable lane — an investigational drug.
Reference entry — NOT a sellable lane, NOT yet approved. Potentially the most consequential drug in this whole register — not because it's the strongest, but because it's the first oral small-molecule GLP-1, which could solve the class's cost-and-supply problem. NDA for obesity submitted Dec 2025. No dosing published here.
Status note: fast-moving — re-check the FDA obesity decision and T2D filing
Evidence provenance: 3 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/orforglipron (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Oxytocin
title: Oxytocin — the "love hormone": approved for labor, unproven for bonding/autism, and the replication story
text:
Oxytocin — peptide.
Also known as: Oxytocin, OXT, trade names Pitocin (IV/IM), Syntocinon (nasal, outside US).
Oxytocin is a 9-amino-acid endogenous hormone with two lives. As a peripheral hormone, it drives uterine contraction and milk let-down — and synthetic oxytocin (Pitocin) is a genuinely FDA-approved obstetric drug for labor induction and postpartum bleeding. As a central neurotransmitter, it shapes maternal and social bonding — which made it famous as the "love/trust/cuddle hormone." That fame outran the data: many marquee human findings (oxytocin boosting trust or empathy/"mind-reading") failed to replicate, and oxytocin became a symbol of the replication crisis. Its biggest therapeutic hope, autism, has largely disappointed — the large SOARS-B trial found no benefit, and a 2022 NEJM editorial summed it up as "down, but not out." Add genuine uncertainty about how much intranasal oxytocin even reaches the brain, and the honest picture is: a real, important, approved hormone for childbirth — and a much-hyped, inconsistently-supported one for the social/emotional uses it's mostly sold and celebrated for. Big, real biology; approved for one thing; oversold for another.
Overall evidence grade: A (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Labor induction / uterotonic / postpartum hemorrhage (the approved use): grade A. Evidence base: FDA-approved (Pitocin); decades of obstetric use; clear OTR→myometrial-contraction mechanism. Caveat: Real, established medicine — but a hospital obstetric drug, nothing to do with the "bonding" uses it's sold for; misuse is dangerous.
- Lactation / milk let-down: grade B. Evidence base: Historically approved (Syntocinon nasal). Caveat: Withdrawn in US for commercial (not efficacy) reasons; still used in some countries.
- Social bonding / trust / empathy (healthy people — the "love hormone" claims): grade D. Evidence base: Strong animal data; human findings largely failed to replicate; effect sizes small. Caveat: The replication-crisis core: early trust/"mind-reading" results didn't hold up; underpowered studies + publication bias.
- Autism spectrum disorder (social deficits): grade D. Evidence base: Decades of RCTs; SOARS-B (large, Phase 2): no benefit; meta-analyses heterogeneous (I²≈93%). Caveat: "Down, but not out" — inconsistent, unreplicated; possible subgroup/dose signals unproven.
- PTSD / anxiety / addiction / social anxiety: grade F. Evidence base: Preliminary human studies. Caveat: Early, mixed; nothing established.
- Safety: grade not graded. Evidence base: Extensive obstetric + research exposure. Caveat: Obstetric IV: risk of uterine hyperstimulation, fetal distress, water intoxication at high/prolonged doses. Intranasal in trials: generally well-tolerated short-term; brain delivery uncertain.
Legal status (US): Approved / prescription. FDA-approved as Pitocin (IV/IM) for obstetric use; the nasal spray is not currently marketed in the US (withdrawn 1995). Intranasal oxytocin for social/psychiatric uses is investigational / off-label / compounded.
Legal status (EU): Approved / prescription. approved as an obstetric drug (oxytocin/carbetocin); Syntocinon nasal spray available in some countries; social/psychiatric uses investigational.
The "love hormone" — approved for childbirth, hyped for everything social, and a textbook replication-crisis story. Oxytocin is a real, approved drug and one of the most over-hyped molecules in popular science. Three threads: (1) as Pitocin, synthetic oxytocin is a genuinely FDA-approved obstetric drug (labor induction, postpartum bleeding) — solid, narrow, real; (2) its fame as the "love/bonding/trust hormone" rests on social-neuroscience findings, many of which failed to replicate; (3) after decades of autism research — including the large SOARS-B trial — intranasal oxytocin has not shown consistent clinical benefit. The gap between the icon and the evidence is the whole entry.
Status note: watch: OXTR-genotype-stratified / precision-medicine trials and any dose/subgroup autism signal — the field's remaining hope after SOARS-B
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/oxytocin (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 & Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5
title: Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 & -5 — evidence, collagen-signal claims & status
text:
Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 & Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5 — peptide.
Also known as: - Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 = Pal-GHK (palmitoylated Gly-His-Lys), trade name Biopeptide CL, trade name SYN-COLL (DSM).
- INCI: as named. (Both are cosmetic INCI ingredients.).
Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1 (Pal-GHK) and Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5 (Pal-KVK / SYN-COLL) are signal peptides proposed, like Matrixyl, to tell skin fibroblasts to make collagen — Tripeptide-1 as a GHK matrikine, Tripeptide-5 by activating TGF-β. They are mechanistically plausible and very well tolerated, but the standalone human evidence is mostly manufacturer/in-vitro (thinner than Matrixyl's single split-face RCT), and most of the human data for Tripeptide-1 is actually for the Matrixyl 3000 combination, not the peptide alone. They are cosmetic ingredients only.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Topical: appearance of fine lines / firmness — Palmitoyl Tripeptide-1: grade C. Evidence base: Mechanistic (GHK matrikine, TGF-β) + Matrixyl 3000 combination studies. Reported effect: Plausible improvement. Caveat: Standalone human RCT is thin; most human data is for the Matrixyl 3000 combo, not the peptide alone.
- Topical: appearance — Palmitoyl Tripeptide-5 (SYN-COLL): grade C. Evidence base: Manufacturer studies + TSP-1/TGF-β mechanism. Reported effect: Preliminary improvement. Caveat: Manufacturer-dominated; preliminary.
- Collagen / ECM stimulation (both): grade C. Evidence base: In-vitro (TGF-β pathway; collagen I/III, fibronectin). Reported effect: Stimulates in vitro. Caveat: In vitro; reported magnitudes are largely manufacturer-derived.
- Safety (both, topical): grade not graded. Evidence base: Broad cosmetic use. Reported effect: Well tolerated, non-irritating. Caveat: Good safety; no photosensitivity; suitable for sensitive skin.
Legal status (EU): Lawful cosmetic. lawful cosmetic ingredients (EU Regulation (EC) 1223/2009; US cosmetic law), sold over-the-counter worldwide. No injectable/research lane. Sellable as cosmetics — the constraint is the claims.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): for the **appearance** of firmer, plumper, smoother skin; helps reduce the **look** of fine lines..
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: boosts / stimulates / increases **collagen**; collagen-boosting; activates TGF-β; rebuilds collagen; by function.
Cosmetic signal peptides — the "collagen-signal" archetype (like Matrixyl #28), with the same claim trap. Two related but distinct collagen-signalling peptides. Mechanistically among the better-grounded cosmetic peptides and very well tolerated — but the standalone human evidence is mostly manufacturer/in-vitro, and the "collagen boosting" claim is the structural-claim trap.
Evidence provenance: 4 cited sources, 0 with a verified DOI.
Reviewer status: unassigned. Grades are Vallydia's own evidence assessment, not third-party certification.
Source: Vallydia Ingredient-Evidence Register, https://vallydia.com/compound/palmitoyl-tripeptide-1-5 (CC-BY-4.0). Reference information about appearance and evidence only; not medical advice.

name: PDRN (Polydeoxyribonucleotide)
title: PDRN — evidence, uses & status
text:
PDRN (Polydeoxyribonucleotide) — blend.
Also known as: polydeoxyribonucleotide, "salmon DNA", salmon / trout sperm DNA fragments, PN (polynucleotides), INCI: sodium DNA / hydrolyzed DNA (cosmetic forms vary).
PDRN (polydeoxyribonucleotide) is a mixture of DNA fragments, usually derived from salmon or trout, marketed as "salmon DNA" skincare. The honest split: as an INJECTED treatment it is a real, well-studied regenerative compound used in clinical and aesthetic settings — but that is a regulated medical procedure, not a serum. The topical "salmon DNA" products riding that reputation are a much gentler thing: PDRN's large molecules mostly cannot cross the skin barrier, so a serum offers soothing, hydration, and post-procedure comfort rather than the dramatic renewal the injections deliver. That is genuinely nice for sensitive and post-procedure skin, but a world away from the miracle framing. Full deep-dive: the PDRN explainer in the Journal.
Overall evidence grade: C (A = strong and consistent human evidence, F = unproven or tested and failed; the grade reflects the leading application).
Evidence by outcome:
- Topical cosmetic serum — soothing, hydration, barrier comfort: grade C. Evidence base: Topical PDRN/polynucleotide cosmetics are humectant and film-forming; the supportable benefits are soothing, hydration, and post-procedure comfort — not the regeneration seen with the injectable.. Reported effect: Gentle soothing, hydration, and barrier comfort. Caveat: PDRN is a large DNA-fragment molecule that mostly cannot cross the intact stratum corneum, so a topical serum does not deliver the regeneration the injected form does. Benefits are real but modest and comfort-focused..
- Topical "regeneration / rejuvenation" (as marketed for salmon-DNA serums): grade D. Evidence base: No evidence that a topical serum reproduces injected-PDRN outcomes; penetration of large polynucleotides through intact skin is doubtful.. Reported effect: Not supported for a topical serum. Caveat: The serum borrows the reputation of the injected drug. Claims of dramatic renewal from a topical PDRN overstate the evidence and ignore the penetration problem..
- Injected PDRN (clinical / aesthetic, in-office) — for context, NOT cosmetic scope: grade not graded. Evidence base: A well-studied regenerative compound as an INJECTED treatment — clinical evidence exists for wound healing, tissue repair, and aesthetic ("salmon DNA") procedures, where it engages adenosine A2A receptors and supplies nucleotides to cells.. Reported effect: Genuine regenerative signalling when injected by a professional. Caveat: This is a medical/aesthetic procedure administered by qualified professionals and regulated as a drug or device in many regions — it is out of cosmetic scope, carries procedural risk, and is NOT what a topical serum delivers. Listed only so the two stories are not confused..
Legal status (INT): Cosmetic topical forms sold as skincare; injectable PDRN is a regulated medical treatment. Topical PDRN/polynucleotide cosmetics are sold over the counter framed around soothing and hydration. Injected PDRN is a medical/aesthetic procedure regulated as a drug or device depending on region and belongs to qualified professionals. Regulatory context, not a health claim.
Permissible cosmetic claims (appearance wording): helps skin feel soothed, calm, and comfortable; provides lightweight hydration and a comforting feel; a gentle option for sensitive or post-procedure-feeling skin; supports the look and feel of a comfortable skin barrier.
Claims that must NOT be made for this cosmetic ingredient: regenerates or renews skin; repairs tissue or wounds; reverses ageing; stimulates collagen or fibroblasts; heals or treats any condition; delivers the results of injectable PDRN.
A sellable topical PDRN product is framed around soothing, hydration, and comfort for sensitive or post-procedure-feeling skin — never regeneration or the outcomes of injectable PDRN. The injected/clinical context below is reference background only and is out of cosmetic scope.
Honest note: PDRN's real regenerative evidence is for the INJECTED drug, not the topical serum. Large PDRN molecules mostly cannot cross intact skin, so topical benefits are limited to soothing, hydration, and barrier comfort. Do not credit a serum with the injectable's results.
Identity: PDRN — polydeoxyribonucleotide — is a mixture of short DNA fragments, most often extracted and purified from salmon or trout (hence the "salmon DNA" nickname). A closely related cosmetic term, PN (polynucleotides), refers to the same family of DNA-fragment ingredients. It is not a single clean molecule but a heterogeneous set of nucleotide chains, which is why this entry files it under a blend rather than one defined compound.

The single most important fact about PDRN is that it lives a double life, and the two versions are easy to confuse: an injected regenerative treatment with genuine clinical evidence, and a topical "salmon DNA" serum that borrows that reputation but works far more gently. This entry is a short, graded reference for the cosmetic (topical) use; the full detective breakdown — including the injectable's story — is in the Journal explainer, the honest guide to PDRN and "salmon DNA" skincare.
